10.1144/gsl.sp.2002.197.01.11.title: The properties, morphology and distribution of igneous sills: modelling, borehole data and 3D seismic from the Faroe-Shetland area
10.1144/gsl.sp.2002.197.01.11.abstract: Abstract An extensive suite of igneous sills, collectively known as the Faroe-Shetland Sill Complex, has been intruded into the Cretaceous and Tertiary sedimentary section of the Faroe-Shetland Channel area. These sills have been imaged offshore by three-dimensional (3D) reflection seismic surveys and penetrated by several exploration boreholes. Data from wireline log measurements in these boreholes allow us to characterize the physical properties of the sills and their thermal aureoles. The borehole data has been compiled to produce new empirical relationships between sonic velocity and density, and between compressional and shear sonic velocities within the sills. These relationships are used to assist in calculation of synthetic seismic traces for sills intruded into sedimentary section, in order to calibrate the seismic response of the sills as observed in field data. This paper describes how the seismic amplitude response of the sills can be used to predict sill thickness where there is some nearby well control, and use this technique to estimate the volume of one well-imaged sill penetrated by Well 205/10-2b. Since the sills have a high impedance contrast with their host rocks, they return strong seismic reflections. 3D seismic survey data allow mapping of the morphology of the sills with a high level of confidence, although in some instances disruption of the downgoing seismic wavefield causes the seismic imaging of deeper sills and other structures to be very poor. Examples of sub-circular and dish-shapes sills, and also semi-conical and sheet-like intrusions, which are highly discordant are shown. The introduction of intrusive rocks can play an important role in the subsequent development of the sedimentary system. An example is shown in which differential compaction or soft sediment deformation around and above the sills appears to have controlled deposition of a reservoir quality sand body. The positioning of the sills within sedimentary basins is discussed, by constructing a simple model in which pressure support of magma from a crustal magma chamber provides the hydrostatic head of magma required for intrusion at shallow levels. This model is made semi-quantitative using a simple equation relating rock densities to intrusion depth, calibrated to observations from the Faroe-Shetland area. The model predicts that sills can be intruded at shallower levels in the sedimentary section above basement highs, which agrees with observations detailed in this paper.

10.1130/g19559.1.title: New technique for dating igneous sills
10.1130/g19559.1.abstract: Research Article| September 01, 2003 New technique for dating igneous sills James Trude; James Trude 1School of Earth, Ocean, and Planetary Sciences, Cardiff University, Cardiff CF10 3YE, UK Search for other works by this author on: GSW Google Scholar Joe Cartwright; Joe Cartwright 1School of Earth, Ocean, and Planetary Sciences, Cardiff University, Cardiff CF10 3YE, UK Search for other works by this author on: GSW Google Scholar Richard J. Davies; Richard J. Davies 2ExxonMobil Exploration Co., 233 Benmar, Houston, Texas 77060, USA Search for other works by this author on: GSW Google Scholar John Smallwood John Smallwood 3Amerada Hess Ltd., 33 Grosvenor Place, London SW1X 7HY, UK Search for other works by this author on: GSW Google Scholar Geology (2003) 31 (9): 813–816. https://doi.org/10.1130/G19559.1 Article history received: 07 Feb 2003 rev-recd: 07 May 2003 accepted: 12 May 2003 first online: 02 Mar 2017 Cite View This Citation Add to Citation Manager Share Icon Share Facebook Twitter LinkedIn MailTo Tools Icon Tools Get Permissions Search Site Citation James Trude, Joe Cartwright, Richard J. Davies, John Smallwood; New technique for dating igneous sills. Geology 2003;; 31 (9): 813–816. doi: https://doi.org/10.1130/G19559.1 Download citation file: Ris (Zotero) Refmanager EasyBib Bookends Mendeley Papers EndNote RefWorks BibTex toolbar search Search Dropdown Menu toolbar search search input Search input auto suggest filter your search All ContentBy SocietyGeology Search Advanced Search Abstract Three-dimensional seismic interpretation of Tertiary sills within the northeast Atlantic margin demonstrates that shallow-level (<1 km) intrusion of igneous sills below the seafloor can deform the interface between water and sediment during its early burial stage by hydraulic elevation of the overburden. We show that a group of interconnected sills forcibly "jacked up" the seafloor, contributing to the creation of a minibasin 5 km wide, 250 m deep, and >20 km long. The fill of the minibasin is dated biostratigraphically as 54.6–55 Ma, which fits well with radiometric dates for the timing of intrusion of sills around this basin (ca. 53–55 Ma). The recognition and dating of onlap-fill seismic reflector packages that are delimited by forced folds form a new and useful method for dating shallow-level igneous intrusions in sedimentary basins, a method that provides independent constraints on radiometric dating techniques. Similar forced folds are recognized from other basins that have shallow-level sills, suggesting that the technique presented here may be similarly applicable in comparable geologic settings. 10.1029/2002gl016658.title: Methane escape from gas hydrate systems in marine environment, and methane‐driven oceanic eruptions
10.1029/2002gl016658.abstract: Huge quantities of CH 4 are stored in marine sediment in the form of methane hydrate, bubbles, and dissolved CH 4 in pore water. Here I discuss the various pathways for methane to enter the ocean and atmosphere, including: (i) Methane hydrate dissolution or dissociation as it rises through seawater. The dissociation rate can be 2 to 3 orders of magnitude greater than the dissolution rate. (ii) The dissolution and expansion of a bubble with or without a hydrate shell as it rises through seawater. There is a critical radius (which depends on depth), above which a bubble would reach the surface or even become larger as it rises. I also propose and model the dynamics of a new type of terrestrial gas‐driven eruptions: methane‐driven oceanic eruptions. Such eruptions not only represent a yet unrecognized geohazard, but also provide a pathway for CH 4 to rapidly enter the atmosphere as a greenhouse gas.

10.1126/science.285.5428.724.title: Mechanisms of Climate Warming at the End of the Paleocene
10.1126/science.285.5428.724.abstract: An abrupt episode of global warming marked the end of the Paleocene epoch. Oxygen and carbon isotope records from two widely separated sites support the notion that degassing of biogenic methane hydrate may have been an important factor in altering Earth's climate. The data show evidence for multiple injections of methane, separated by intervals in which the carbon cycle was in stasis. Correlations between the two sites suggest that even these small-scale events were global in nature.

Geology 1996;; 24 (5): 423–426. doi: https://doi.org/10.1130/0091-7613(1996)024<0423:RDOPFI>2.3.CO;2 Download citation file: Ris (Zotero) Refmanager EasyBib Bookends Mendeley Papers EndNote RefWorks BibTex toolbar search Search Dropdown Menu toolbar search search input Search input auto suggest filter your search All ContentBy SocietyGeology Search Advanced Search Abstract The planktonic foraminiferal genera Morozovella and Acarinina rapidly (in ∼10 k.y.) diversified during the late Paleocene thermal maximum (LPTM), giving rise to such morphotypes as M. allisonensis (new species), M. africana, and A. sibaiyaensis. Single-specimen isotopic analysis confirms that M. allisonensis and A. sibaiyaensis are restricted to the LPTM carbon isotope excursion recorded at Ocean Drilling Program Site 865 (equatorial Pacific Ocean). The short-lived (50 to several 100 k.y.) "excursion" taxa attest to the ephemeral effects of the LPTM on the calcareous plankton. Single-specimen oxygen isotope data show that evolution of M. allisonensis and A. sibaiyaensis was accompanied by migration to deeper water depths. Ancestral M. velascoensis and A. soldadoensis were extremely rare or absent during the early stages of the LPTM, but immigrated back into the study area to coexist with their descendants in later LPTM horizons. Photosymbiosis may have facilitated the morozovellid and acarininid diversifications during the oligotrophic conditions of the LPTM. This content is PDF only. Please click on the PDF icon to access. J. Bralower, D. J. Thomas, J. C. Zachos, M. M. Hirschmann, U. Röhl, H. Sigurdsson, E. Thomas, D. L. Whitney; High-resolution records of the late Paleocene thermal maximum and circum-Caribbean volcanism: Is there a causal link?. Geology 1997;; 25 (11): 963–966. doi: https://doi.org/10.1130/0091-7613(1997)025<0963:HRROTL>2.3.CO;2 Download citation file: Ris (Zotero) Refmanager EasyBib Bookends Mendeley Papers EndNote RefWorks BibTex toolbar search Search Dropdown Menu toolbar search search input Search input auto suggest filter your search All ContentBy SocietyGeology Search Advanced Search Abstract Two recently drilled Caribbean sites contain expanded sedimentary records of the late Paleocene thermal maximum, a dramatic global warming event that occurred at ca. 55 Ma. The records document significant environmental changes, including deep-water oxygen deficiency and a mass extinction of deep-sea fauna, intertwined with evidence for a major episode of explosive volcanism. We postulate that this volcanism initiated a reordering of ocean circulation that resulted in rapid global warming and dramatic changes in the Earth's environment. This content is PDF only. 10.1029/2003gc000631.title: Abrupt hydrographic changes in the equatorial Pacific and subtropical Atlantic from foraminiferal Mg/Ca indicate greenhouse origin for the thermal maximum at the Paleocene‐Eocene Boundary
10.1029/2003gc000631.abstract: The Paleocene‐Eocene Boundary (PEB) was marked by an extraordinary climatic event, hypothesized to originate from a large perturbation to the carbon cycle which fueled global warming, the rapid dissociation of oceanic methane hydrates. The pattern of surface warming interpreted from existing sea surface temperature records is not consistent with a greenhouse origin for this event, which would have fueled sea surface warming globally. Although oxygen isotope (δ 18 O)‐based reconstructions indicate polar warming, results for the tropics and subtropics are ambiguous because of uncertainties associated with interpreting planktonic foraminiferal δ 18 O. To remedy this, we have constructed high‐resolution temperature records based on Mg/Ca of multiple species of both surface and thermocline‐dwelling planktonic foraminifera across the PEB in the equatorial Pacific and subtropical Atlantic. During the carbon isotope excursion (CIE), surface temperatures increased by 3.5°–4°C and thermocline temperatures warmed by 3°C. Estimates of surface water and thermocline salinity based on paired Mg/Ca and δ 18 O data indicate a pattern of hydrographic changes in the equatorial and subtropical oceans that is different from previously proposed, with a more vigorous hydrologic cycle during warming. The pattern of warming and salinity changes are consistent with this being a greenhouse‐induced global warming event, and the timing of hydrographic changes relative to the CIE supports the hypothesis that gradual warming of intermediate/deep waters triggered methane hydrate dissociation.

10.1029/2002pa000761.title: Response of Antarctic (ODP Site 690) planktonic foraminifera to the Paleocene–Eocene thermal maximum: Faunal evidence for ocean/climate change
10.1029/2002pa000761.abstract: High‐resolution study of Antarctic planktonic foraminiferal assemblages (Ocean Drilling Program Site 690, Weddell Sea) shows that these microplankton underwent a stepwise series of changes during the Paleocene–Eocene thermal maximum (PETM). Initiation of this response coincides with the onset of the carbon isotope excursion (CIE) but precedes the benthic foraminiferal mass extinction. The “top‐to‐bottom” succession in the biotic response indicates that the surface ocean/atmosphere was affected before the deep sea. The earliest stage of the faunal response entailed a conspicuous turnover within the shallow‐dwelling genus Acarinina and a succession of stratigraphic first appearances. The genus Morozovella , large (>180 μm) biserial planktonics, and A. wilcoxensis are all restricted to the lower CIE within this PETM section. Acarininid populations crashed as the ocean/climate system ameliorated during the CIE recovery, reflecting atypical surface water conditions. This transient decline in acarininids is paralleled by a marked increase in carbonate content of sediments. It is postulated that this interval of carbonate enrichment, and its unusual microfauna, reflects enhanced carbon storage within reservoirs of the global carbon cycle other than the marine carbonate system [sensu Broecker et al. , 1993 ; Ravizza et al. , 2001 ].

10.1029/1999pa900031.title: New evidence for subtropical warming during the Late Paleocene thermal maximum: Stable isotopes from Deep Sea Drilling Project Site 527, Walvis Ridge

authors.name: Deborah J Thomas
authors.institution: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

authors.name: Timothy J. Bralower
authors.institution: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

10.1029/1999pa900031.abstract: The late Paleocene thermal maximum (LPTM) was a dramatic, short‐term global warming event that occurred ∼55 Ma. Warming of high‐latitude surface waters and global deep waters during the LPTM has been well documented; however, current data suggest that subtropical and tropical sea surface temperatures (SSTs) did not change during the event. Conventional paradigms of global climate change, such as CO 2 ‐induced greenhouse warming, predict greater warming in the high latitudes than in the tropics or subtropics but, nonetheless, cannot account for the stable tropical/subtropical SSTs. We measured the stable isotope values of well‐preserved late Paleocene to early Eocene planktonic foraminifera from South Atlantic Deep Sea Drilling Project (DSDP) Site 527 to evaluate the subtropical response to the climatic and environmental changes of the LPTM. Planktonic foraminiferal δ 18 O values at Site 527 decrease by ∼0.94‰ from pre‐LPTM to excursion values, providing the first evidence for subtropical warming during the LPTM. We estimate that subtropical South Atlantic SSTs warmed by at least ∼1°–4°C, on the basis of possible changes in evaporation and precipitation. The new evidence for subtropical SST warming supports a greenhouse mechanism for global warming involving elevated atmospheric CO 2 levels.

10.1130/0091-7613(1996)024<0347:lpbeeo>2.title: Latest Paleocene benthic extinction event on the southern Tethyan shelf (Egypt): Foraminiferal stable isotopic (δ13C, δ18O) records

authors.name: M. -P. Aubry
authors.institution: Institut des Sciences de l'Evolution de Montpellier

10.1130/0091-7613(1996)024<0347:lpbeeo>2.abstract: The dramatic global extinction of 35%-50% of benthic foraminifera species in the deep sea in the latest Paleocene and associated negative excursions in delta(13)C and delta(18)O may be related to spreading of warm, saline bottom water from subtropical Tethyan shallow regions over the sea floor worldwide, Our study of neritic sections in Egypt shows that in the southern shallow Tethys, a prominent long-term change in bottom-water chemistry, sedimentation, and benthic foraminifera fauna was initiated at the time when the deep-sea benthic extinction event (BEE) took place, Bottom-water delta(13)C values on the Tethyan shelf show a sudden 3.0 parts per thousand negative shift at this event; however, contrary to the deep sea, in which the delta(13)C excursion was of short duration, Tethyan delta(13)C values did not fully return to preboundary values, but remained depressed by similar to 1.5 parts per thousand for at least 1 m.y. The delta(13)C values at the Egyptian shelf during the BEE are much lower than would be expected if this was a source region for global deep water, The delta(18)O values indicate no significant change in bottom-water salinity or temperature at the BEE. The long-lasting environmental changes that began on the Egyptian shelf at the BEE may be related to, for example, gateway reorganization along the Tethyan seaway. Paleogeographic changes possibly also triggered a change in the Loci of global deep-water formation; however, these loci must be sought in another part of the Tethys.

10.1016/0377-8398(95)00039-9.title: Differential patterns of benthic foraminiferal extinctions near the Paleocene/Eocene boundary in the North Atlantic and the western Tethys

10.1130/g24474a.1.title: Sedimentary response to Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum carbon release: A model-data comparison
10.1130/g24474a.1.abstract: Research Article| April 01, 2008 Sedimentary response to Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum carbon release: A model-data comparison K. Panchuk; K. Panchuk 1Department of Geosciences, Pennsylvania State University, University Park, Pennsylvania 16802, USA Search for other works by this author on: GSW Google Scholar A. Ridgwell; A. Ridgwell 2School of Geographical Sciences, University of Bristol, University Road, Bristol BS8 1SS, UK Search for other works by this author on: GSW Google Scholar L.R. Kump L.R. Kump 3Department of Geosciences, Pennsylvania State University, University Park, Pennsylvania 16802, USA Search for other works by this author on: GSW Google Scholar Geology (2008) 36 (4): 315–318. https://doi.org/10.1130/G24474A.1 Article history received: 01 Oct 2007 rev-recd: 17 Dec 2007 accepted: 19 Dec 2007 first online: 03 Mar 2017 Cite View This Citation Add to Citation Manager Share Icon Share Facebook Twitter LinkedIn MailTo Tools Icon Tools Get Permissions Search Site Citation K. Panchuk, A. Ridgwell, L.R. Kump; Sedimentary response to Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum carbon release: A model-data comparison. Geology 2008;; 36 (4): 315–318. doi: https://doi.org/10.1130/G24474A.1 Download citation file: Ris (Zotero) Refmanager EasyBib Bookends Mendeley Papers EndNote RefWorks BibTex toolbar search Search Dropdown Menu toolbar search search input Search input auto suggest filter your search All ContentBy SocietyGeology Search Advanced Search Abstract Possible sources of carbon that may have caused global warming at the Paleocene-Eocene boundary are constrained using an intermediate complexity Earth-system model configured with early Eocene paleogeography. We find that 6800 Pg C (δ13C of –22‰) is the smallest pulse modeled here to reasonably reproduce observations of the extent of seafloor CaCO3 dissolution. This pulse could not have been solely the result of methane hydrate destabilization, suggesting that additional sources of CO2 such as volcanic CO2, the oxidation of sedimentary organic carbon, or thermogenic methane must also have contributed. Observed contrasts in dissolution intensity between Atlantic and Pacific sites are reproduced in the model by reducing bioturbation in the Atlantic during the event, simulating a potential consequence of the spread of low-oxygen bottom waters. You do not have access to this content, please speak to your institutional administrator if you feel you should have access.

10.1016/j.epsl.2010.04.044.title: Ocean acidification and surface water carbonate production across the Paleocene–Eocene thermal maximum

10.1029/2006pa001395.title: Reversed deep‐sea carbonate ion basin gradient during Paleocene‐Eocene thermal maximum
10.1029/2006pa001395.abstract: The Paleocene‐Eocene thermal maximum (PETM, ∼55 Ma ago) was marked by widespread CaCO 3 dissolution in deep‐sea sediments, a process that has been attributed to massive release of carbon into the ocean‐atmosphere system. The pattern of carbonate dissolution is key to reconstructing changes in deep sea carbonate chemistry and, ultimately, the rate, magnitude, and location of carbon input. Here we show that during the PETM, the deep‐sea undersaturation was not homogeneous among the different ocean basins. Application of a sediment model to a suite of data records from different sites and ocean basins shows that a globally uniform decrease in deep‐sea carbonate ion concentration ([CO 3 2− ]) is inconsistent with the data. Rather, we demonstrate that deep‐sea [CO 3 2− ] increased from the Atlantic through the Southern Ocean into the Pacific. Our results show that the PETM deep‐sea [CO 3 2− ] basin gradient during dissolution was reversed relative to the modern.

10.1016/j.margeo.2011.02.009.title: Impact of the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum on the macrobenthic community: Ichnological record from the Zumaia section, northern Spain

10.1038/s41561-023-01246-8.title: Shallow-water hydrothermal venting linked to the Palaeocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum
key_findings: Shallow-water hydrothermal vents in the North Atlantic Igneous Province erupted during the Palaeocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM), directly releasing methane and other volatiles into the atmosphere without significant oxidation to CO2

Stable carbon isotope stratigraphy and dinoflagellate cyst biostratigraphy (Apectodinium augustum) firmly tie the Modgunn Vent infill to the PETM onset, with vent formation occurring only a few millennia before the carbon isotope excursion
The funnel-shaped crater morphology and stratified sediment infill indicate rapid explosive gas release during initial vent formation followed by swift, largely undisturbed diatomite-rich infill

High-resolution 3D seismic data document over 700 potential hydrothermal vent complexes on the mid-Norwegian margin alone, with their shallow-water settings significantly amplifying climate impact compared to deep-sea venting

The shallow marine to near-coastal paleoenvironmental conditions during PETM are evidenced by coastal marine diatom and dinoflagellate assemblages, with water depths constrained below wave base (~30+ metres)
10.1038/s41561-023-01246-8.methodology: IODP Expedition 396 drilled five boreholes (U1567A–C and U1568A–B) through a hydrothermal vent complex crater on the Modgunn Arch, Norway, combined with high-resolution 3D seismic interpretation. Biostratigraphy (dinoflagellate cysts and diatoms), stable carbon isotope analysis of bulk organic matter, and paleoenvironmental reconstruction from microfossil assemblages were used to date and characterize vent activity during the PETM.
limitations: Operational safety precautions prevented drilling at the centre of the vent, so signatures of high-temperature fluid alteration may be confined to central and deeper parts not sampled
The recovery phase of the carbon isotope excursion appears absent at the Modgunn sites, limiting precision on the full duration of crater infill
The overall amount and speciation of carbon emitted by NAIP sill emplacement is not constrained beyond previous estimates, and CH4/CO2 ratios in hydrothermal volatiles are not directly measured
future_work: Further coring of additional NAIP hydrothermal vent complexes to assess the role and magnitude of thermogenic degassing on the Palaeocene–Eocene carbon cycle across broader spatial scales
Investigation of other shallow-water HTVC systems globally to constrain the temporal correlation between vent activity and climate perturbations at continental margins
Detailed geochemical modelling to quantify CH4/CO2 ratios and volatile emission rates from shallow-water hydrothermal systems and their atmospheric impact
Study of other large igneous provinces in the geological record to evaluate whether shallow-water hydrothermal venting may have triggered previous warming and extinction events

10.1029/2023gl102897.title: Strong Coupling Between Carbon Cycle, Climate, and Weathering During the Paleocene‐Eocene Thermal Maximum
key_findings: High-resolution lacustrine lithium isotope measurements reveal an ~100% increase in regional silicate weathering intensity during the PETM compared to pre-PETM levels
Strong coupling between carbon isotope variations and weathering intensity demonstrates synchronous responses of carbon cycle, climate, and chemical weathering on millennial timescales
Negative δ7Li excursions in both carbonate and clay records indicate more congruent silicate weathering during the PETM, with the proportion of dissolved Li increasing ~2.5 times at peak PETM
Weathering intensity shows significant correlation with δ13C carbonate values (R² = 0.703, p < 0.0001), indicating ~70% of weathering variance is explained by carbon release processes
Strong negative weathering feedback and massive CO₂ removal via enhanced silicate weathering helped maintain Earth's climate within a habitable range during the PETM thermal crisis
10.1029/2023gl102897.methodology: High-resolution sampling of lacustrine sediments from the Nanyang Basin (eastern China) across the PETM section, with lithium isotope analysis (δ7Li) of carbonate and clay fractions, coupled with carbon isotope (δ13C) measurements and a quantitative box model to calculate basin-scale silicate weathering intensity.
limitations: Limited to single lacustrine basin record; regional applicability to other PETM sections requires further validation
pH effects on δ7Li fractionation during carbonate formation cannot be robustly assessed due to uncertainty in paleolake pH reconstruction
Box model calculations depend on assumptions about clay mineral fractions and erosional flux proportions that may vary
Millennial-scale sampling resolution may miss finer-scale temporal variations in weathering-carbon coupling
Uncertainties in paleoclimate reconstructions and estimated carbon input magnitudes during the PETM
future_work: Expand lacustrine δ7Li records to additional PETM sections globally to assess spatial heterogeneity in weathering responses
Develop improved methods to constrain paleolake pH and better quantify pH effects on carbonate δ7Li signatures
Integrate silicate weathering data with more sophisticated Earth system models to assess feedback mechanisms at millennial timescales
Investigate whether similar weathering-climate-carbon couplings occurred during other rapid carbon release events in Earth history
Apply findings to evaluate the efficacy and timescales of enhanced rock weathering as a climate mitigation strategy for anthropogenic CO₂

10.1016/j.palaeo.2024.112234.title: Paleoenvironment reconstruction of the eastern Tethys during the pre-onset excursion preceding the PETM
key_findings: Compliant joint-equipped motion stages exhibit much more linear closed loop dynamics and higher bandwidth compared to traditional rigid stages when using the same PID controller gains
Compliant stages achieve 85% faster settling times for most point-to-point positioning tasks, with up to 98% reduction when combined with a disturbance observer

The compliant joint method reduces sensitivity of combined stiffness to variations in friction stiffness by a factor of (η/(1+η))² where η = kj/kf, enabling robust compensation with low-fidelity friction models

Addition of an inverse-model-based disturbance observer (DOB) at 200 Hz bandwidth enables the compliant stage to consistently outperform the rigid stage (with 100 Hz DOB) across all tested step sizes, eliminating the problematic 50 μm step performance issue
Bearing motion during settling in compliant stages can be effectively monitored and compensated through disturbance observation, improving robustness and stability margins compared to rigid stages
10.1016/j.palaeo.2024.112234.methodology: An experimental investigation using an ultra-precision mechanical-bearing-guided motion stage with and without compliant joints, evaluating performance through frequency-domain analysis (FRFs) and time-domain point-to-point positioning tests with PID feedback control and disturbance observer compensation.
limitations: Compliant joint design may introduce Abbe errors to ultra-precision stages, though this is not addressed in the paper
PID controller tuned based on gross motion regime FRF, limiting adaptability to varying friction dynamics
Stability analysis uses linearized LTI approximations at different operating points rather than global nonlinear stability guarantees
Study limited to single-axis (1D) motion stage; multi-axis performance not evaluated
Nominal plant model (second-order) may not capture all unmodeled dynamics at higher frequencies, limiting DOB bandwidth selection
future_work: Develop theoretical models of mechanical-bearing-guided motion stages with pre-motion frictional dynamics to explain why bearing motion varies significantly with step size (e.g., 50 μm case)
Provide guidance on optimal parameter selection for compliant joints (stiffness and damping properties)
Explore compliant joint designs with tunable stiffness and damping that enables low stiffness during motion and high stiffness during settling for improved in-position stability
Address Abbe errors introduced by current compliant joint design through embedding joints into table pockets
Extend evaluation to multi-axis (XY) precision positioning stages
themes: Feedback linearization and disturbance observer control

10.1029/2023pa004756.title: Large Igneous Province Control on Ocean Anoxia and Eutrophication in the North Sea at the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum
key_findings: Paleoceanographic reconstructions using high-resolution geochemical data from North Sea core E-8X reveal that redox, productivity, and basin restriction changes occurred before and after the carbon isotope excursion (CIE) during the PETM.

Rapid onset of anoxia/euxinia at the PETM CIE onset is documented by multiple redox proxies (Mo isotopes, pyrite framboid sizes, Fe/Al ratios), with a transition from oxic/suboxic to euxinic bottom water conditions at ~2,025.4 m core depth.
Molybdenum/TOC ratios show gradual drawdown during the PETM main phase, indicating tectonically-restricted basin conditions likely driven by North Atlantic Igneous Province (NAIP) uplift.

Two short-lived precursor events (E1 and E2) before the main PETM CIE show coupling between volcanic activity (indicated by Hg/TOC pulses), productivity increases (TOC), and oxygen depletion, suggesting pulsed NAIP volcanism triggered local oceanographic feedbacks.

The North Sea basin remained anoxic/suboxic for at least ~140 kyr through the recovery phases, never fully recovering to pre-PETM oxic conditions, indicating prolonged effects of NAIP-related tectonism and basin restriction.
10.1029/2023pa004756.methodology: High-resolution geochemical analysis of marine sediment core E-8X from the Danish North Sea including X-ray fluorescence (XRF), molybdenum isotope measurements, pyrite framboid size analysis via scanning electron microscopy, clay mineralogy via X-ray diffraction, and carbon isotope stratigraphy. Samples were analyzed at ~1 cm resolution across the PETM interval with intensified sampling around the CIE onset.
limitations: Limited core recovery at some depth intervals and potential coring disturbance at the PETM onset in previous records; E-8X was selected specifically for undisturbed recovery.
Paleobathymetry estimates for E-8X are inferred from regional comparisons rather than direct paleontological analysis of this specific core.
High BIT index values and associated uncertainties complicate interpretation of pre-PETM sea surface temperature proxy data.
Age model uncertainties due to two cyclostratighraphic options (140 ka vs 200 ka) from the Svalbard record affect precise timing of events.
Mo isotope interpretations rely on assumed global seawater composition values that may have varied regionally during the PETM.
future_work: Future modeling studies should assess the role of the North Sea and nearby basins as local and relative carbon sinks during the PETM and their contribution to global carbon cycling perturbations.
Testing the LIP-triggered biogeochemical feedback hypothesis in other LIP-proximal shelf seas to understand cascade of trigger-response mechanisms leading to global hyperthermal events.
Further investigation of basin restriction mechanisms and the relative contributions of tectonic uplift versus sea level changes to North Sea hydrography during PETM recovery phases.
Higher-resolution temporal sampling to better constrain the timing relationships between volcanic pulses, carbon release, and oceanographic responses.
Integrated paleoclimate modeling combining NAIP emplacement, tectonic uplift, and volcanic gas emissions with ocean biogeochemistry models.
themes: Large Igneous Province volcanism and tectonics

10.1016/j.orggeochem.2024.104899.title: Lacustrine records of Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) triggered by volcanic activity
key_findings: Effervescent SiO2-drug-Na2CO3 composite nanoparticles (ESNs) with a core-shell lipid coating successfully achieved pH-triggered controllable release of hydrophobic drugs (berberine and evodiamine) with simultaneous carrier decomposition
ESNs demonstrated high entrapment efficiency and drug loading capacity, with particle sizes around 294.9 nm and showed excellent stability in vitro for at least 7 days at 4°C

Drug-loaded nanoparticles exhibited 3-fold lower IC50 values against MCF-7 breast cancer cells compared to free drugs, with the LEVO/BBR (6:1) formulation achieving only 9.61% survival rate at 50 μg/mL
pH-dependent drug release was driven by CO2 gas generation from Na2CO3 reacting with H+ ions, with complete disintegration observed at pH 5.0 (tumor cell lysosomes) within 14 days

The delivery system showed successful applicability to multiple hydrophobic drug pairs (COP-EVO and LUT-C3G), demonstrating the universal applicability of the ESN platform for synergistic dual drug delivery
10.1016/j.orggeochem.2024.104899.methodology: Effervescent SiO2-drug-Na2CO3 composite nanoparticles were synthesized using a tetraethyl orthosilicate hydrolysis method and coated with a lipid bilayer membrane. The system was characterized by TEM, particle size analysis, HPLC, and evaluated for drug release kinetics, cellular cytotoxicity, migration, and apoptosis in MCF-7 breast cancer cells using MTT assays, wound-healing assays, and flow cytometry.
limitations: The study only evaluated in vitro cytotoxicity and cellular responses; in vivo experiments in animal models were not conducted
Limited to testing on a single breast cancer cell line (MCF-7); biocompatibility was only tested on HEK-293 and HUVEC cells
The paper does not address potential toxicity from residual sodium carbonate after carrier decomposition in vivo
Long-term biodegradation profiles and systemic elimination kinetics were not comprehensively studied
The study does not evaluate potential immune responses or off-target effects in complex biological environments
future_work: Conduct in vivo pharmacokinetic and biodistribution studies in animal tumor models to validate therapeutic efficacy and safety
Investigate the impact of pH-triggered CO2 generation on lysosomal integrity and cellular organelle function
Explore targeting strategies (antibodies, peptides) to enhance preferential accumulation at tumor sites
Evaluate the combination of ESNs with other therapeutic modalities (radiotherapy, phototherapy) for enhanced synergistic effects
Develop formulations with alternative effervescent agents and optimize drug ratios for different cancer types beyond breast cancer

10.1029/2024gl113117.title: Assessing the Duration of the Paleocene‐Eocene Thermal Maximum
key_findings: The Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) carbon isotope excursion (CIE) duration is significantly longer than previously estimated, measuring 268.8 +21.2/-20.5 kyr compared to prior estimates of ~120-230 kyr
The traditional 'G point' approach for identifying PETM CIE termination is unreliable because it is sensitive to underlying carbon isotope uncertainties and noise in δ13C records

Exponential decay functions reveal consistent e-folding timescales of ~35-43 kyr for PETM CIE recovery at multiple global sites (Contessa Road, ODP Sites 1209, 1262, and 1266), reflecting accelerated organic carbon removal compared to silicate weathering

A new probabilistic-based detection limit protocol (0.6%-2.2% interval) provides a statistically defined method to constrain the time range over which the PETM CIE is detectable in stable carbon isotope records

The protracted PETM CIE recovery duration (>145 kyr) demonstrates that extreme carbon cycle perturbations cause long-lasting disruptions to the natural carbon cycle, with implications for anthropogenic carbon cycle impacts
10.1029/2024gl113117.methodology: The authors applied Monte Carlo-based probabilistic uncertainty assessments and statistical analyses to high-resolution δ13C records from six globally distributed PETM sedimentary sections (Bighorn Basin, Contessa Road, and ODP Sites 690, 1209, 1262, 1266). Exponential decay functions were fitted to CIE recovery intervals to assess carbon removal trajectories, and bootstrap resampling was used to generate single numerical estimates with confidence intervals.
limitations: Exponential decay functions do not adequately fit PETM CIE recovery patterns at Bighorn Basin and ODP Site 690 (χ² > ~2.7), which were excluded from final analysis due to noisy δ13C records and regional controls
ODP Sites 1262 and 1266 lack δ13C data at the CIE onset due to extensive CaCO3 dissolution caused by ocean acidification
The new duration estimate may be modified by inclusion of additional well-resolved δ13C records
The approach for estimating age model uncertainties varied across sites due to contrasting measurement resolutions, with average half age spacing used as proxy where uniform spacing was unavailable
Detailed comparison between PETM and anthropogenic carbon e-folding timescales is complicated by differences in carbon injection rates and model setup conditions
future_work: Integration of additional well-resolved δ13C records from other locations to refine the global PETM CIE duration estimate
Development of improved carbon cycle models that can reproduce the ~35-43 kyr e-folding timescales observed in geological records
Comparison of PETM carbon cycle recovery mechanisms with projections for anthropogenic carbon cycle disruption timescales under different climate scenarios
Investigation of regional controls on δ13C records to explain variations in CIE recovery patterns between terrestrial and marine sites
Further analysis of the mechanisms driving the protracted PETM CIE recovery, including the roles of organic carbon burial, chemical weathering, and biological pump efficiency
themes: Carbon isotope excursion (CIE) duration and recovery
Stable carbon isotope (δ13C) records and paleoclimate
Carbon cycle perturbations and feedback mechanisms
Statistical and probabilistic methods in paleoclimate reconstruction

10.5194/sd-33-47-2024.title: Paleogene Earth perturbations in the US Atlantic Coastal Plain (PEP-US): coring transects of hyperthermals to understand past carbon injections and ecosystem responses
key_findings: The Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) involved release of over 4500 Gt of carbon with a global temperature increase of ~5°C, making it the closest geological analog to modern anthropogenic CO2 emissions

US Atlantic Coastal Plain sediments, particularly the Marlboro Clay, contain expanded PETM records (>15 m thick) with sedimentation rates 1-2 orders of magnitude higher than deep-sea deposits, enabling submillennial temporal resolution

Evidence of in situ impact spherules at the PETM onset in multiple coastal plain sites and deep-sea records, along with abundant charcoal peaks, suggests a potential extraterrestrial trigger for widespread wildfires and carbon release
The Pre-Onset Excursion (POE) represents a distinct ~2 kyr warming event preceding the main PETM, with evidence of ocean acidification and temperature rise prior to the main carbon isotope excursion

Multiple Eocene hyperthermals (H1, H2, I1, I2, J, K, L) occurring after the PETM were astronomically paced and provide additional analogs for rapid warming events at different climate background states
10.5194/sd-33-47-2024.methodology: This work presents a comprehensive drilling workshop report proposing 10 strategically located coring sites across the US mid-Atlantic Coastal Plain (New Jersey, Delaware-Maryland, and Virginia) to recover expanded Paleocene-Eocene records. The study integrates well log data, existing core material, seismic interpretation, and isopach mapping to target thick PETM sections for high-resolution paleoclimate and ecosystem reconstruction.
limitations: Age control is historically challenging in coastal plain sediments due to sparse microfossils traditionally used for biostratigraphy

Differential preservation and patchwork distribution of PETM sediments in coastal deposits makes target intervals difficult to predict; some proposed sites in Delaware-Maryland pose higher risk of missing target strata due to limited well data control

Carbonate dissolution zones at many sites preclude stable isotope analysis at the Paleocene-Eocene contact; existing cores from Bass River and Millville are largely depleted from intensive community sampling
Stratigraphic gaps due to local faulting and erosion in some cores (e.g., Knapps Narrows) complicate correlation between sites
The precise duration of the CIE onset remains unresolved, with estimates ranging widely from nearly instantaneous to ~4 kyr
future_work: Develop high-resolution chronologies using bulk δ13C records, zircon and apatite geochronology, XRF scanning, and impact spherules as isochronous marker horizons to establish stratigraphic framework and inter-site correlation

Conduct detailed paleodepth reconstructions using backstripping models, lithofacies analysis, benthic foraminifera, ostracods, and planktonic foraminifera to constrain relative sea level rise and test mantle dynamic topography effects
Investigate causes of the CIE onset by testing relationships between carbon release, impact events, and wildfires using integrated δ13C, charcoal, and microtektite analyses across the transect

Analyze hydrological cycle changes through hydrogen isotope analysis of leaf wax n-alkanes and mineralogical/isotopic provenance studies to constrain sediment source regions and paleodrainage patterns

Quantify biotic effects of PETM, POE, and Eocene hyperthermals through multi-proxy community studies of nannofossils, planktonic/benthic foraminifera, and dinoflagellate cysts to identify ecological tipping points
themes: Carbon cycle perturbations and carbon isotope excursions
Coastal plain paleoceanography and shelf ecosystems
Rapid climate change and hyperthermal events

10.1029/2023pa004773.title: Spatial and Temporal Patterns in Petrogenic Organic Carbon Mobilization During the Paleocene‐Eocene Thermal Maximum
key_findings: Enhanced petrogenic organic carbon (OCpetro) mobilization occurred in subtropical and mid-latitude regions during the PETM, with increases in mass accumulation rates ranging from 2×10⁻² to 7×10⁻² gC cm⁻² Kyr⁻¹
High-latitude sites (Arctic ACEX and ODP Site 1172) showed limited changes in organic carbon sources, suggesting spatially variable feedback mechanisms
OCpetro mass accumulation rates remained elevated during the recovery phase of the PETM at several sites (Ancora and SDB), suggesting continued CO₂ release beyond the core interval
Extreme rainfall events in subtropical and mid-latitudes, indicated by episodic high variability in thermal maturity ratios and chain-length distributions, likely drove enhanced OCpetro mobilization

Lipid biomarker thermal maturity ratios (hopanes and n-alkanes) successfully tracked changes in organic carbon sources, with the two-endmember mixing model quantifying OCpetro fractions across a global compilation of seven PETM-aged shallow marine sites
10.1029/2023pa004773.methodology: Lipid biomarker analysis using gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) was applied to sediment samples from seven PETM-aged shallow marine sites. Thermal maturity ratios derived from n-alkanes (CPI) and hopanes (multiple isomerization ratios) were used with a two-endmember mixing model to quantify petrogenic organic carbon fractions and calculate mass accumulation rates (MARs).
limitations: Limited geographic coverage of high-latitude sites (only two sites) may not fully represent global high-latitude responses
Constant dry bulk density value (1.8 g cm⁻³) was assumed across all sites due to limited published data, which may introduce uncertainty in absolute MAR estimates
Hopane distributions vary depending on lithofacies and depositional environment, requiring caution when comparing thermal maturity ratios between sites with different source rock characteristics
Exact timing of OCpetro delivery remains unclear due to irregular sampling resolution and potential core recovery gaps at some sites
Uncertainty regarding the proportion of OCpetro that was oxidized versus preserved in sediments, ranging from ~10-40% in modern systems
future_work: Apply Raman spectroscopy to identify oxidation efficiency based on the degree of degradable versus recalcitrant organic carbon
Develop paleo-digital elevation models to provide insight into sediment routing systems and transit times during the PETM
Conduct simultaneous quantification of both biospheric and petrogenic organic carbon to determine whether regions were net carbon sources or sinks
Extend high-latitude site sampling to better constrain global responses of OCpetro mobilization and determine if current patterns represent fundamental latitudinal differences
Investigate negative feedback mechanisms (silicate weathering, OCbio burial) required to explain climate recovery despite continued OCpetro oxidation into the recovery phase
themes: Petrogenic organic carbon (OCpetro) mobilization and oxidation
Hydrological cycle and extreme precipitation events
Positive feedback mechanisms in past climate warming

10.5194/cp-19-1623-2023.title: Tracing North Atlantic volcanism and seaway connectivity across the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM)
key_findings: Peak North Atlantic Igneous Province (NAIP) volcanism occurred during the body of the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM), spanning approximately 200 thousand years (56.0-55.8 Ma), dominated by effusive volcanism and thermogenic degassing with mercury accumulation rates an order of magnitude greater than modern volcanic contributions

NAIP eruption style shifted from effusive volcanism and thermogenic degassing during the PETM to explosive phreatomagmatic activity in the early Eocene, driven by magma-water interactions in developing rift systems rather than increased magma production

Rapid basin isolation of the North Sea from the Atlantic Ocean occurred at the PETM-recovery boundary in less than 12 thousand years (55.82 Ma), triggered by NAIP-driven thermal uplift that closed the English Channel gateway

Continental silicate weathering of volcanic ash deposits increased dramatically during the PETM, creating a negative feedback mechanism that enhanced carbon sequestration and contributed to terminating the warming phase

Osmium isotope ratios and clay mineralogy confirm continuous mantle-derived material input during the PETM body, while the post-PETM shift to radiogenic osmium values at Fur indicates seaway restriction distinct from open-ocean conditions in the Arctic
10.5194/cp-19-1623-2023.methodology: This study employs integrated geochemical and paleoenvironmental proxy analysis on an expanded sedimentary sequence from Stolleklint Beach on Fur, Denmark. The research utilizes mercury concentrations, osmium isotope ratios, lithium isotope analysis, clay mineralogy, and thaumarchaeotal lipid biomarkers (GDGT distributions) to constrain NAIP volcanic activity timing, style, and environmental impacts across the Paleocene-Eocene boundary.
limitations: The North Sea basin's intermittent connections to multiple oceans (Arctic, Atlantic, Tethys) complicate interpretation of seawater connectivity signals
Uncertainty remains regarding the precise contributions of different carbon sources (volcanic CO2, thermogenic methane, methane hydrate destabilization) to the PETM warming
Limited availability of similar expanded sedimentary records containing both volcanic and climatic proxies in other geographic locations restricts global comparison
Dating constraints on ash layers rely on radiometric ages with associated uncertainties
The apparent paradox between declining volcanic markers during the post-PETM and the abundance of ash layers requires further clarification of eruption style changes
future_work: Incorporate additional radiometric dating constraints on key ash layers to further refine NAIP activity chronology
Compile similar detailed proxy analyses from other locations proximal to large igneous provinces to establish global NAIP activity patterns
Conduct further investigation of the mechanisms driving the shift from effusive to phreatomagmatic eruption styles during the Paleocene-Eocene
Apply these methodologies to other large igneous province emplacement events to test the generality of NAIP-climate interactions
Develop higher-resolution records of seawater circulation changes during basin restriction to better constrain ocean gateway closure timing
themes: Volcanic Carbon Cycling and Weathering Feedbacks

10.5194/cp-7-831-2011.title: Down the Rabbit Hole: toward appropriate discussion of methane release from gas hydrate systems during the Paleocene-Eocene thermal maximum and other past hyperthermal events
key_findings: Massive carbon isotope excursion (δ13C) during PETM requires input of 2000-12000 Gt of carbon depending on isotopic composition, with methane hydrate dissociation remaining the most congruous but unproven explanation

Multiple early Eocene hyperthermal events (ETM2/H1, H2, I1, I2, K/X) show patterns coupled to orbital forcing and appear to share similar generic causes with the PETM, suggesting a large dynamic carbon reservoir is needed

Gas hydrate dissociation would operate as a positive feedback to climate change, with warming preceding carbon input by at least 1000 years, and primary impact being oxidation of CH4 to CO2 in ocean or atmosphere

Current estimates of present-day marine gas hydrate carbon mass range from 170-12700 Gt C depending on methodology, with significant uncertainties in GHSZ volume, gas hydrate saturation, and total carbon (including dissolved gas and free gas)

An alternative coupled carbon-sulfur cycle model suggests gas hydrate systems could explain both the δ13C excursions and the observed δ34S changes during the early Paleogene, with sulfide minerals accumulating through anaerobic oxidation of methane
10.5194/cp-7-831-2011.methodology: The author conducts a comprehensive review and re-evaluation of existing geological data, carbon isotope records, mass balance calculations, and numerical models to assess the viability of methane hydrate dissociation as the cause of the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum carbon isotope excursion. The analysis incorporates sedimentological, geochemical, and paleoceanographic evidence from multiple ocean drilling program sites and published literature.
limitations: The gas hydrate dissociation hypothesis lacks definitive positive proof despite being congruent with available data; it is based entirely on circumstantial evidence and consistency with observations
Current knowledge of present-day marine gas hydrate reservoir mass is poorly constrained, with estimates varying by over two orders of magnitude across different modeling approaches
Uncertainties in the timing and magnitude of δ13C and δ34S excursions, and the masses and fluxes in coupled carbon-sulfur cycle models are largely best guesses with limited field constraints
No good estimates exist for global fluxes of carbon passing in and out of modern gas hydrate systems, making it difficult to validate paleogenic models
The fundamental problem of what initially drove deep-sea warming at the start of the PETM remains unresolved and is critical to evaluating feedback mechanisms
future_work: Determine whether the carbon input associated with the δ13C excursion was a positive feedback to warming rather than the primary driver by examining environmental change sequences
Develop model simulations of gas hydrate and free gas distribution in early Paleogene conditions with warmer bottom water, lower dissolved O2 and SO42−, and faster methanogenesis rates
Test the hypothesis that gas hydrate systems operated as a dynamic carbon capacitor by examining predicted effects on δ13C recovery rates following massive CH4 release

Provide and test compelling alternative explanations for PETM carbon inputs that are consistent with available geological data, including large terrestrial organic carbon reservoirs or permafrost sources

Examine sedimentary records on continental slopes for evidence of changes in CH4 outputs and inputs during the Late Paleocene and Early Eocene, as this is critical to validating gas hydrate involvement

10.1002/palo.20039.title: What caused the long duration of the Paleocene‐Eocene Thermal Maximum?
key_findings: Heat conduction calculations demonstrate that temperature anomalies generated at the sediment-water interface require tens of thousands of years (10-26 kyr for 100-500 m depth) to penetrate deep-sea sediments, causing a long tail of methane hydrate dissociation beyond the initial 10 kyr warming phase

Slow methane release from methane hydrate dissociation in subsurface sediments, combined with reduced hydrate deposition and enhanced sedimentary metabolic processes, can explain the 50+ kyr duration of the PETM that conventional carbon cycle models fail to reproduce with a single initial carbon pulse

The calculated composite methane dissociation flux shows continued carbon release substantially beyond the initial warming phase, with fluxes dropping to only 4% of the initial rate after ~30 kyr, consistent with paleoceanographic observations and previous carbon cycle modeling constraints

Multiple temperature-dependent carbon sources (methane hydrate dissociation, anaerobic oxidation of methane, sulfate reduction, and enhanced metabolic mineralization) all release carbon depleted in ¹³C, making an overall source composition of -50‰ plausible to match observed carbon isotope excursions

Water column remineralization in response to 5 K warming could increase atmospheric CO₂ by ~200 ppmv, potentially helping resolve the PETM climate sensitivity conundrum where carbon input alone appears insufficient to explain the observed warming
10.1002/palo.20039.methodology: Using first-principles heat transfer calculations with the thermal diffusion equation and numerical modeling (Crank-Nicolson scheme), the author simulates temperature anomaly propagation into marine sediments in response to the 5 K bottom water warming during the PETM, then calculates methane hydrate dissociation fluxes at multiple water depths (1000-3200 m) accounting for latent heat uptake and hydrate stability zone dynamics.
limitations: Hydrate dissociation flux calculations based on simplified assumptions about hydrate depth distribution and sediment pore space concentration, which are poorly known
Large uncertainties in modern estimates of global metabolic rates in marine sediments complicate extrapolation to past conditions
Model focuses only on marine processes without considering potential terrestrial carbon sources during the PETM
Study does not include fully interactive feedback between methane release, atmospheric greenhouse gas concentrations, surface warming, and further methane release
Estimated contribution from lack of hydrate deposition (~5% of total bleeding flux) and sulfate reduction alkalinity effects (~40 ppmv CO₂ reduction) appear relatively modest
future_work: Develop fully coupled climate-carbon cycle models that include heat transport in sediments and interactive feedbacks between methane hydrate dissociation, atmospheric composition changes, and ocean warming
Improve constraints on hydrate inventory, concentration in sediments, and spatial distribution through enhanced observational efforts
Better quantify global metabolic rates of sedimentary organic carbon remineralization and temperature sensitivities for past climates
Investigate terrestrial carbon sources and processes that may have contributed to prolonged carbon input during the PETM
Force coupled sediment-climate models with observed paleoceanographic records to directly constrain carbon input rates over time
themes: Methane hydrate dissociation and gas hydrate systems
Heat transfer and thermal diffusion in marine sediments
Carbon cycle feedbacks and carbon isotope excursions

10.5194/cp-10-1421-2014.title: Warming, euxinia and sea level rise during the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum on the Gulf Coastal Plain: implications for ocean oxygenation and nutrient cycling
key_findings: Continental and sea surface temperatures warmed by approximately 5-8°C during the PETM on the Gulf Coastal Plain, with continental air temperatures rising from 27-29°C to ~35°C
Photic zone euxinia (anoxic and sulfidic conditions) developed seasonally along the Gulf margin during the PETM, indicated by the presence of sulfur-bound isorenieratene biomarker

Sea level rose during the PETM, contradicting previous hypotheses of sea level fall; terrigenous sediment deposition shifted landward and glauconite-rich sediments accumulated due to sedimentary condensation
Ocean deoxygenation expanded to continental margins globally during the PETM, with widespread seasonal anoxia documented across the Arctic, Tethys margins, North Sea, and Pacific regions

Enhanced phosphorus regeneration from anoxic sediments likely drove shelf eutrophication and increased organic carbon burial, creating a negative feedback to carbon cycle perturbations and global warming
10.5194/cp-10-1421-2014.methodology: Multiproxy stratigraphic and paleoclimatologic study of late Paleocene and early Eocene sediments from the Harrell Core in Mississippi, utilizing carbon and oxygen isotope analysis, biomarker-based paleotemperature proxies (TEX86, MBT-CBT), dinoflagellate cyst and pollen assemblages, calcareous nannofossil analysis, and organic matter geochemistry to reconstruct climate, ocean oxygenation, and depositional environments across the PETM.
limitations: Absolute temperature estimates may be underestimated due to high supply of terrestrial soil lipids affecting TEX86 calibration
Uncertainty in dating the T4 sand channel at the Red Hot Truck Stop and in determining which portion of the PETM body is preserved in the Harrell Core due to erosional boundaries
Lack of quantitative data on absolute phosphorus fluxes and organic carbon burial rates to fully assess biogeochemical feedback mechanisms
Limited data points for isorenieratene presence restrict definitive interpretation of spatial and temporal distribution of euxinic conditions
Palynological assemblage quantification in upper Paleocene samples complicated by dilution of marine dinoflagellate cysts by terrigenous material
future_work: Quantify changes in nutrient (particularly phosphorus) budgets and oxygen concentrations during the PETM across multiple ocean basins to improve understanding of biogeochemical feedbacks
Determine the timescales on which phosphorus regeneration feedback operates and its relationship to organic carbon burial rates during the event
Establish more precise correlations between anoxic zone expansion and primary productivity changes to validate eutrophication hypotheses
Improve mid- to long-term projections of anthropogenic ocean deoxygenation impacts by using the PETM as an analogue for rapid environmental change
Further investigate the mechanisms driving the global success and ecological controls on Apectodinium abundance across latitudinal gradients
themes: Sea level change and coastal environments

10.1029/2011gb004224.title: Assessing offsets between the δ13C of sedimentary components and the global exogenic carbon pool across early Paleogene carbon cycle perturbations
key_findings: The magnitude of the carbon isotope excursion (CIE) in total organic carbon (δ¹³C_TOC) across the PETM is exaggerated by approximately 3‰ relative to marine organic matter due to increased terrestrial organic carbon contributions during the event
Linear regression analysis reveals strong correlation (R² = 0.91) between δ¹³C_TOC and the BIT index, a proxy for relative terrestrial versus marine organic matter contributions

The calculated CIE in marine organic matter (δ¹³C_MOM) across the PETM is approximately 3.5-4‰, smaller than the 6‰ observed in bulk TOC, suggesting the true global exogenic carbon cycle CIE is likely <4‰

Three main factors bias individual δ¹³C records: changes in relative abundance of carbon components with different isotopic values, physiological changes affecting isotope fractionation, and changes in isotope composition of the carbon source

Early Paleogene marine organic matter was approximately 7‰ more depleted in ¹³C compared to Holocene values, consistent with higher past pCO₂ leading to increased carbon isotope discrimination during photosynthesis
10.1029/2011gb004224.methodology: The study analyzes δ¹³C records from IODP Hole 302-4A (Lomonosov Ridge, Arctic Ocean) spanning the late Paleocene to early Eocene (~58-52 Ma) using linear regression to correlate total organic carbon isotope values with proxies for terrestrial/marine organic matter composition (BIT index and palynomorphs). This framework is then applied to other published δ¹³C records to assess substrate effects on carbon isotope excursions during hyperthermal events.
limitations: Terrestrial and marine organic matter end-member values are derived from a single location (Arctic Ocean) and may not apply universally to all depositional settings
The BIT index may not scale linearly with total terrestrial organic matter abundance in marine sediments
Palynomorph percentages show weaker correlation (R² = 0.48) with δ¹³C_TOC compared to BIT, likely due to taphonomic differences in transport of soil biomarkers versus pollen
Corrections account only partially for changes in marine biota composition during hyperthermals, which may have altered relative proportions of marine organic carbon components
The study cannot fully constrain fractionation factors (equation 2) and local carbon pool offsets (equation 3) needed to completely remove substrate bias from all records
future_work: Additional analyses and modeling are needed to obtain accurate values for the true CIE magnitude of the global exogenic carbon cycle during the PETM and other hyperthermals
Future work should apply the three-equation framework to constrain changes in fractionation factors and local carbon pool offsets across multiple δ¹³C records from different substrates and locations
High-resolution records of deep ocean δ¹³C (where 85-95% of exogenic carbon resides) should be generated to better constrain whole-system carbon cycle changes
Studies should investigate how changing marine biota and vegetation composition affected carbon isotope fractionation during hyperthermals

Systematic examination of the framework for other major climate perturbations in Earth's history (Paleozoic excursions, Permian-Triassic, Triassic-Jurassic, Mesozoic anoxic events, K-Pg boundary) is recommended
themes: Sedimentary proxy records and substrate bias
Early Paleogene climate change and hyperthermals

10.1029/2019gl084818.title: Assessing the Contributions of Comet Impact and Volcanism Toward the Climate Perturbations of the Paleocene‐Eocene Thermal Maximum
key_findings: A multiproxy geochemical analysis (osmium isotopes, mercury, sulfur, and platinum group elements) from North Atlantic Ocean drill cores provides evidence that both a comet impact (~3.3 km diameter) and volcanism occurred at the Paleocene-Eocene boundary

The comet impact is estimated to contain approximately 0.4 Gt of carbon, which is far less than the 3,000-10,000 Gt required to drive the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum, indicating the comet alone cannot explain the carbon influx

Climate modeling simulations indicate that stratospheric sulfate aerosols from the estimated comet impact would have caused transient global cooling (sea surface temperature decrease of ~2.2°C, land temperature decrease of ~3.5°C) and a 7% reduction in global precipitation within 2 years

Mercury enrichments at both study sites correlate with elevated clay content and show consistent elevation patterns suggesting a volcanic source from North Atlantic Igneous Province activity rather than a single pulse impact signature
Osmium isotope excursions shifted toward unradiogenic values at the Paleocene-Eocene boundary, consistent with input from either volcanic activity or extraterrestrial impact sources
10.1029/2019gl084818.methodology: The researchers conducted a multiproxy geochemical analysis of two North Atlantic Ocean drill cores (Millville and Blake Nose) using Re-Os isotope analysis, platinum group element (PGE) measurements, mercury analysis, pyrite sulfur extraction, and total organic carbon (TOC) determination. They complemented geochemical data with Earth system model (WACCM-CARMA) climate simulations to assess the potential climatic effects of a stratospheric sulfate aerosol injection from the estimated comet impact.
limitations: The exact magnitude of the osmium isotope excursion attributable to impact versus volcanism remains unclear, making precise impactor size estimation difficult
No definitive platinum group element anomalies were detected at the Paleocene-Eocene boundary, preventing definitive identification of an extraterrestrial impactor signature
The mercury enrichments show different patterns between sites and cannot be unambiguously attributed to volcanism versus sedimentological controls on mercury concentration
The climate model simulations use modern boundary conditions rather than Late Paleocene conditions, which may not fully capture paleoclimate responses
No depositional evidence has been recovered for the episodic cooling and reduced precipitation predicted by the impact simulations
future_work: Investigation of higher temporal resolution sedimentary records to identify potential evidence of episodic cooling and reduced precipitation resulting from the comet impact
Further analysis of additional Paleocene-Eocene boundary sections to constrain the global nature and magnitude of mercury enrichment patterns and their sources
Investigation of whether impact events may have triggered secondary carbon release mechanisms such as methane hydrate destabilization or vaporization of hydrocarbon-rich target sediments
Refinement of climate model simulations using Late Paleocene boundary conditions to improve accuracy of predicted climate perturbations from impact-derived sulfate aerosols
Additional studies to determine the relative contributions of volcanism versus impact events to the osmium isotope record and overall environmental perturbations
themes: Geochemical proxies (osmium, mercury, platinum group elements)
Paleoclimate modeling and Earth system dynamics

10.1029/2010gl046038.title: Methane and environmental change during the Paleocene-Eocene thermal maximum (PETM): Modeling the PETM onset as a two-stage event
key_findings: The PETM onset can be modeled as a two-stage event: Stage 1 (first 3 kyr) characterized by atmospheric methane release of 900-1100 Pg C with δ¹³C of -22 to -30‰, and Stage 2 (1 kyr) involving mixed emissions of 900-1400 Pg C total (300-500 Pg C CO₂ to ocean and 400-900 Pg C CH₄ to atmosphere) with δ¹³C of -50 to -60‰
Stage 1 results cannot be associated with any known PETM hypothesis, requiring either rapid organic carbon release or thermogenic CH₄ sources, suggesting current PETM hypotheses may require revision
Stage 2 results are consistent with catastrophic methane hydrate dissociation, with 30-45% of total carbon release oxidized in the ocean and the remainder escaping to atmosphere as CH₄
Carbon release must have occurred over at most 500 years (50 years optimal) to explain observations, with purely CO₂ releases unable to reproduce Stage 1 characteristics
Multiple carbon sources are required to explain the PETM onset, indicating at least two distinct emission events with different isotopic signatures and rates
10.1029/2010gl046038.methodology: A coupled atmospheric CH₄ box model and global carbon cycle box model were used to simulate carbon emission scenarios representing different amounts, rates, and isotopic signatures. The models were tuned to pre-PETM conditions and run with a range of CO₂ and CH₄ emission scenarios (up to 5000 Pg C resolution), with multiple emission durations and locations tested against proxy-derived target characteristics including carbon isotope excursion (CIE), lysocline shoaling, and temperature change.
limitations: Uncertainty in paleoclimate proxy estimates (temperature change ranging from 1-7°C depending on stage) and climate sensitivity parameter values (0.5-1.0°C W⁻¹ m⁻²) introduce substantial constraints on emission estimates
Assumptions about the temporal partition of carbonate dissolution between stages (0.5 km in Stage 1, 1.5 km in Stage 2) remain uncertain and affect results
Stage 1 characteristics do not correspond to any known PETM hypothesis, leaving the true carbon source unidentified
Model results are sensitive to stage length assumptions (difficult to explain targets for 5 and 10 kyr Stage 1 durations)
Alternative CO₂ emission locations (Indian, Pacific, thermocline) produce no Stage 2 simulations consistent with Atlantic lysocline targets
future_work: Further investigation of Stage 1 carbon sources, including assessment of rapid organic carbon combustion (biomass burning) and thermogenic CH₄ release rates
Improved temporal constraints from high-resolution geochemical records to better distinguish between single and multiple-stage carbon release events
Refinement of paleoclimate proxy estimates and climate sensitivity parameters to reduce uncertainty in carbon emission quantification
Investigation of alternative mechanisms for the pre-CIE warming and carbonate dissolution observed in the geological record
Integration of additional geochemical tracers and modeling approaches to better constrain methane hydrate dissociation versus other carbon sources

10.1029/2018pa003425.title: Widespread Warming Before and Elevated Barium Burial During the Paleocene‐Eocene Thermal Maximum: Evidence for Methane Hydrate Release?
key_findings: Global sea surface warming of ~2°C preceded the PETM carbon isotope excursion (CIE) by approximately 2 millennia, indicating that CO2-driven warming triggered a positive carbon cycle feedback
Oceanic barium (Ba) burial rates increased by a factor of ~3 during the PETM on average across continental shelf, slope, and deep ocean settings, requiring an additional source of Ba to the ocean

Evidence from multiple geographic locations (equatorial Atlantic Site 959, New Jersey Shelf, Southern Ocean Maud Rise) consistently shows that sea surface temperature warming led the CIE onset, supporting a global pre-CIE warming signal

The magnitude of pre-CIE warming (~2°C) is similar to warming projected for the coming centuries, highlighting the potential for strong positive carbon cycle feedbacks even under relatively low amplitude warming

Methane hydrate dissociation triggered by early warming likely acted as a strong positive carbon cycle feedback, releasing dissolved barium-rich pore waters and causing the observed increase in global Ba burial rates
10.1029/2018pa003425.methodology: High-resolution carbon isotope (δ13C) and sea surface temperature (TEX86) analysis of marine sediment cores from ODP Site 959 (equatorial Atlantic) combined with data from New Jersey Shelf and Southern Ocean sites. Cross-correlation functions were used to quantify lead-lag relationships between warming and the carbon isotope excursion, with global barium burial rates compiled from published and new data across shelf, slope, and deep ocean settings.
limitations: The exact temporal relation between barium increase and the CIE remains elusive due to lack of high-resolution Ba and δ13C records suitable for lead-lag analysis
Coverage is limited primarily to Atlantic Ocean regions; no high-resolution records available from Indo-Pacific sectors
Uncertainties in barium accumulation rate estimates may not fully capture global heterogeneity in Ba accumulation
Potential secondary remobilization and reprecipitation of barite in some sediments cannot be completely excluded
Better dating and volumetric assessment of volcanic activity on <100 kyr timescales for the North Atlantic Igneous Province are needed
Limited evidence exists for permafrost thawing as a carbon source during the late Paleocene
future_work: Obtain better dating and volumetric assessments of volcanic deposits and activity in the North Atlantic Igneous Province on sub-100 kyr timescales
Develop suitable modeling to clarify how early Cenozoic sulfate concentrations affected barium distribution, saturation, and burial patterns
Generate high-resolution synchronized Ba and δ13C records to enable detailed lead-lag analysis between barium burial changes and the carbon isotope excursion
Expand paleoclimate data coverage to Indo-Pacific sectors to better constrain global pre-CIE warming patterns
Investigate the mechanisms of barium transport from shelf/slope settings to open ocean sediments under different oceanographic conditions
Reconsider the temperature sensitivity of methane hydrates relative to current climate change scenarios and the safety of 2°C warming targets

10.3389/fevo.2023.1226349.title: Modeling hyperthermal events in the Mesozoic-Paleogene periods: a review
10.3389/fevo.2023.1226349.short_summary: This document is a system-generated error message indicating that the DOI (Digital Object Identifier) "10.3389/fevo.2023.1226349" could not be found in the DOI registry.
