10.3389/fevo.2023.1226349.methodology: Not specified - full paper content not available

10.1016/j.gloplacha.2023.104175.title: Freshwater ecosystem collapse and mass mortalities at the Paleocene-Eocene thermal maximum
10.1016/j.gloplacha.2023.104175.short_summary: This study examines the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) using a lacustrine record from eastern China. It identifies a rapid ∼11°C warming and a concurrent ∼−5‰ carbon isotope excursion. The research links this extreme warming to mass mortalities of terrestrial and aquatic animals, proposing that toxigenic cyanobacterial blooms, fueled by the hyperthermal conditions, were the primary cause. These findings highlight a direct connection between abrupt global warming and catastrophic ecological disruptions in freshwater ecosystems, offering a critical ancient analogue for understanding potential biotic responses to contemporary anthropogenic climate change.
key_findings: A ~11°C rapid temperature increase during the PETM is documented through carbonate clumped isotope (Δ47) analysis in a lacustrine section from the Jianghan Basin, eastern China
Both total organic carbon and bulk carbonate record a synchronous ~5‰ negative carbon isotope excursion (CIE), indicating major carbon cycle perturbation

Mass mortalities of terrestrial and aquatic animals coincided with rapid warming during the PETM, with taphonomic and geochemical evidence pointing to toxigenic cyanobacterial blooms as the primary cause

Hyperthermal-induced harmful cyanobacterial blooms appear responsible for ecosystem collapse in the freshwater lacustrine setting, establishing a direct link between extreme warming and catastrophic ecological events
The Longwangjing section preserves exquisite fossils in well-laminated black shales, providing rare high-resolution paleoenvironmental records of terrestrial ecosystem response to PETM warming
10.1016/j.gloplacha.2023.104175.methodology: The study integrates geochemical analysis (δ13C analysis of total organic carbon and bulk carbonates, clumped isotope paleotemperatures from Δ47), sedimentological observations, taphonomic analysis of fossil assemblages, and biostratigraphy on a continuous ~10 m lacustrine section spanning the Paleocene-Eocene boundary from the Jianghan Basin in eastern China.
limitations: Limited discussion of the global generalizability of findings from a single lacustrine basin location
Reliance on paleontological interpretation of cyanobacterial blooms rather than direct fossil evidence of cyanobacteria themselves
Constraints on well-preserved terrestrial PETM records that are typically lacking in the geological record
future_work: Comparison with other terrestrial PETM sections to assess whether cyanobacterial bloom-induced mass mortality was a widespread phenomenon during this hyperthermal event
Investigation of the mechanisms controlling cyanobacterial bloom toxicity and bloom dynamics during rapid warming episodes
Enhanced study of other well-preserved terrestrial PETM localities to better understand ecosystem responses across different paleoenvironments and paleoclimate regions
themes: Carbon isotope excursion and carbon cycle perturbation
Cyanobacterial blooms and harmful algal events
Terrestrial ecosystem response to climate change

10.1016/j.epsl.2007.04.024.title: The Paleocene–Eocene carbon isotope excursion in higher plant organic matter: Differential fractionation of angiosperms and conifers in the Arctic
10.1016/j.epsl.2007.04.024.short_summary: This study investigates the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) using biomarkers from Arctic Ocean sediments. It finds that angiosperms and conifers responded differently to the climate event: angiosperm biomarkers show a carbon isotope excursion (CIE) of ~6‰, while conifer biomarkers show only ~3‰. This differential fractionation explains the previously observed ~4.5‰ CIE in terrestrial n-alkanes as a mixed signal. The results indicate that the large and variable CIE in terrestrial records partly reflects shifts in vegetation composition and distinct physiological responses of plant groups to increased temperature and humidity during the PETM.
key_findings: Conifers and angiosperms showed differential carbon isotope fractionation during the Paleocene-Eocene thermal maximum (PETM), with conifers exhibiting a 3‰ negative carbon isotope excursion (CIE) versus 6‰ for angiosperms
The previously reported 4.5‰ CIE in terrestrial C29 n-alkanes represents an averaged signal of mixed conifer and angiosperm inputs rather than a true atmospheric CO2 signal

Angiosperm biomarkers (tetra-aromatic triterpenoid) increased in relative abundance at the end of PETM despite pollen analysis showing a relative decrease in angiosperm pollen, indicating differential preservation or production patterns
Conifer-derived biomarkers (dehydroabietane and simonellite) showed CIE magnitudes (~3‰) comparable to marine carbonate records, suggesting conifers better reflected atmospheric CO2 changes
The differential physiological responses of angiosperms and conifers to PETM warming, humidity increases, and elevated greenhouse gases altered their carbon isotopic fractionation patterns
10.1016/j.epsl.2007.04.024.methodology: Molecular isotopic analysis of terrestrial biomarkers from sediments recovered from the Lomonosov Ridge in the Arctic Ocean using GC/MS and stable carbon isotope analysis to examine angiosperm-specific and conifer-specific organic compounds across the Paleocene-Eocene boundary.
limitations: The study relies on a single Arctic Ocean drilling site, limiting geographic generalization
Stratigraphic positioning of Core 31X is uncertain due to recovery as core-catcher material
Pollen and biomarker records show conflicting trends that are not fully reconciled in the paper
The mechanism explaining differential isotopic fractionation between plant groups during PETM warming is not definitively established
future_work: Multi-site comparison of plant-specific biomarker isotope records to test geographic generalizability of the differential fractionation pattern
Further investigation into the physiological mechanisms causing increased carbon isotopic fractionation in angiosperms versus conifers under elevated temperature and CO2
Reconciliation of discrepancies between biomarker and pollen abundance records during PETM
Application of modern calibrations of n-alkane production rates to better constrain paleovegetation composition from biomarker assemblages

10.30909/vol.06.01.129145.title: Carbon release from Large Igneous Province magmas estimated from trace element-gas correlations
10.30909/vol.06.01.129145.short_summary: This study presents a novel method for estimating carbon dioxide (CO₂) release from Large Igneous Provinces (LIPs), major volcanic events linked to past climate changes. The approach uses an empirical correlation between trace element ratios (Sr/Sm, Sr/Nd) in volcanic rocks and volcanic gas CO₂/S ratios, derived from modern volcanoes, which is then applied to geochemical data from ancient LIPs. Results indicate that CO₂ concentrations in LIP magmas vary by 1–2 orders of magnitude, with particularly high levels in alkaline suites like those in the Siberian Traps. This variability suggests that changing magma chemistry and flux can drive significant fluctuations in CO₂ outgassing, which is crucial for understanding how LIP volcanism impacts climate and the carbon cycle.
key_findings: CO2 concentrations in Large Igneous Province (LIP) tholeiitic magmas span 1080-7471 ppm depending on province, with the Siberian Traps being the most CO2-rich (~4500 ppm) and the Deccan Traps the most CO2-poor (~1500 ppm)
CO2/S ratios and therefore CO2 concentrations vary by 1-2 orders of magnitude within individual LIP magma suites, indicating substantial heterogeneity and systematic evolution through magmatism
Alkaline LIP magmas contain CO2/S ratios that are a factor of 5-10 higher than coeval tholeiitic magmas, with parental CO2 contents ranging from 10,225-23,598 ppm for alkaline Siberian Traps rocks

The empirical correlation between whole-rock Sr/Sm and Sr/Nd ratios and gas CO2/S ratios from modern volcanoes can be applied to LIP rocks to reconstruct primary CO2 contents when melt inclusions are sparse or degassed

Evidence suggests CO2 evolution through LIP magmatism, with preliminary analysis of the Columbia River Basalts indicating higher CO2 in early Steens magmas (~0.7-1.4 wt%) declining to lower CO2 in Grande Ronde magmas (~0.3-0.4 wt%)
10.30909/vol.06.01.129145.methodology: Empirical approach using observed correlations between trace element ratios (Sr/Sm and Sr/Nd) in whole-rock volcanic samples from six major Phanerozoic LIPs and modern volcanoes with corresponding gas CO2/S ratios. New major and trace element geochemical data for alkaline Siberian Traps rocks were combined with published data compiled from geological databases to estimate parental magma CO2 contents by scaling CO2/S ratios to melt inclusion sulfur measurements.
limitations: The empirical calibration is based on only 12 modern volcanoes with high-temperature gas composition data, limiting statistical robustness
Melt inclusion sulfur data are sparse or absent for many LIP units, requiring extrapolation from limited measurements within each province
Crustal contamination can modify Sr/Sm and Sr/Nd ratios, potentially obscuring correlations with gas CO2/S ratios
CO2 and S may be fractionated during magmatic degassing due to dissimilar solubilities, and closed-system degassing is a limited condition in mafic volcanic systems
Deep open-system processes in LIP plumbing systems and variable sulfide saturation during submarine eruptions could alter CO2/S ratios differently than in modern calibration volcanoes
The approach does not capture CO2 heterogeneity from deep mantle reservoirs unrelated to mantle metasomatism
Analytical uncertainties in trace element data are 1-10% and sulfur concentrations in melt inclusions range from ~2-10%, though extrapolation uncertainties may be larger
future_work: Obtain additional melt inclusion sulfur and CO2 data spanning complete stratigraphies of LIP formations to better constrain temporal evolution of volatile compositions
Characterize CO2 in the proposed high-3He/4He deep mantle source to assess how this reservoir influences volatile systematics in LIPs
Expand the modern volcano calibration dataset beyond 12 volcanoes to improve empirical relationships and reduce statistical uncertainty
Investigate volatile enrichment processes in subcontinental lithospheric mantle (SCLM) to understand the geochemical signature of alkaline LIP magmas
Test hypotheses linking CO2 evolution in early LIP magmatism (Steens Formation) with documented mid-Miocene warming using integrated paleoclimate and geochemical data

10.5194/bg-6-1755-2009.title: A dynamic climate and ecosystem state during the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum: inferences from dinoflagellate cyst assemblages on the New Jersey Shelf
10.5194/bg-6-1755-2009.short_summary: This study analyzes dinoflagellate cyst assemblages from two expanded Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) sections on the New Jersey Shelf. It finds highly variable cyst assemblages during the PETM, contrasting with the stable, uniform patterns seen at other global sites. By integrating dinocyst data with environmental proxies, the authors infer that the PETM involved not only warming but also dynamic regional changes, including sea-level rise and increased freshwater runoff beginning ~10 kyr after the onset. The work concludes that massive carbon release led to a dynamic and variable climate state, at least regionally, challenging the view of the PETM as a period of uniform extreme warmth.
key_findings: Dinoflagellate cyst assemblages on the New Jersey Shelf show significant variability during the PETM, in contrast to stable Upper Paleocene assemblages, indicating a dynamic climate state driven by carbon input

Fresh-water runoff increased approximately 10 kyr after the onset of the carbon isotope excursion, evidenced by the abundance of low-salinity-tolerant taxa (Senegalinium complex) and correlation with magnetic susceptibility
The Apectodinium acme was likely controlled by nutrient/food supply variations rather than single environmental factors, with cyclic patterns potentially related to precession-paced climate cycles
A regional transgression is documented during the PETM, consistent with global sea level rise, evidenced by changes in the Areoligera complex and sediment grain size correlations
The new species Florentinia reichartii and other taxa show temporal abundance peaks throughout the PETM, suggesting environmental heterogeneity and oscillations in surface water conditions
10.5194/bg-6-1755-2009.methodology: Palynological analysis of dinoflagellate cyst assemblages from two stratigraphically expanded PETM sections (Bass River and Wilson Lake, New Jersey Shelf) combined with statistical methods including Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Canonical Correspondence Analysis (CCA), integrated with previously published environmental proxy data including TEX86 paleothermometry, magnetic susceptibility, and sedimentary grain size analysis.
limitations: Limited terrestrial palynomorph data hampers evaluation of changes in terrestrial material flux to the New Jersey Shelf
Fossil record likely represents only a small percentage of surface water assemblages, making complete food web reconstruction difficult

The morphological plasticity of dinoflagellate cysts and their multiple potential morphotypes complicates paleontological taxonomy and makes determination of true biological/ecological entities uncertain
Study represents regional-scale data from New Jersey Shelf; unclear whether documented climate variability during PETM is regional or reflects larger-scale phenomena
future_work: Analysis of additional chronostratigraphically well-calibrated, high-accumulation rate marine and terrestrial PETM sites from around the world to determine if New Jersey climatic variability is regional or global in scale
Further investigation into the identity and feeding ecology of Apectodinium and the mechanism of global eutrophication during the PETM
Detailed study of the hydrological regime and temperature variations at different seasons to better understand climate dynamics during the PETM
Formal description of other newly recorded dinocyst taxa identified in this study and their paleoenvironmental significance
themes: Carbon isotope excursion and carbon cycling

10.5194/cp-15-217-2019.title: Mercury anomalies across the Palaeocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum
10.5194/cp-15-217-2019.short_summary: This study investigates mercury (Hg) anomalies as a proxy for North Atlantic Igneous Province (NAIP) volcanism across the Palaeocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM). Analyzing Hg concentrations and total organic carbon (TOC) in five continental shelf sections, the authors find significant Hg/TOC anomalies near the NAIP (e.g., North Sea, Denmark, Svalbard), but not in more distant locations (New Jersey, Arctic Ocean). This spatial variance suggests Hg was deposited regionally, likely from submarine volcanic and hydrothermal activity. The evidence indicates NAIP magmatism contributed to initiating the PETM, but the continuation of volcanism post-recovery implies a complex relationship between large igneous province activity and hyperthermal climate events.
key_findings: Mercury anomalies show strong spatial variation across PETM sections, with the greatest concentrations (up to 90,100 ppb) proximal to the North Atlantic Igneous Province in the Grane field, North Sea
Hg/TOC anomalies are most prevalent prior to the CIE onset and during the CIE recovery phase, coinciding with abundant tephra layers indicating volcanic activity

Magnitude of Hg/TOC anomalies correlates positively with proximity to the NAIP, suggesting regional rather than global mercury distribution linked to phreatomagmatic eruptions and submarine hydrothermal vent complexes
NAIP magmatism likely triggered the PETM through a positive carbon cycle feedback, evidenced by volcanic proxies appearing ~18-23 kyr before the CIE onset
Diagenesis and organic matter sourcing significantly impact Hg/TOC ratios and must be better constrained to definitively link mercury anomalies to volcanic activity
10.5194/cp-15-217-2019.methodology: Measured mercury concentrations, total organic carbon (TOC) content, and δ¹³C values across five continental shelf sections around the NAIP during the Palaeocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum using pyrolytic analysis and isotope ratio mass spectrometry, comparing results with other volcanic tracers including tephra layers and osmium isotope ratios.
limitations: Incomplete core recovery at Lomonosov Ridge prevented assessment of key intervals including the CIE onset and recovery phases
Low TOC concentrations at Bass River and Dababiya localities create uncertainties in Hg/TOC ratio calculations above recommended thresholds
Post-depositional processes (diagenesis, dissolution, weathering) are poorly understood for mercury and may overprint primary volcanic signals
Variations in organic matter source and degree of maturation between sites could mute or amplify Hg/TOC anomalies independently of volcanic activity
Uncertainty about how different emplacement styles of magmatism (surface volcanism vs. sill intrusions) produce mechanistically different Hg and carbon release patterns
future_work: Conduct source-specific mercury analyses in various organic reservoirs to better understand how different organic matter types affect Hg/TOC relationships
Develop better constraints on how diagenetic processes and dissolution impact Hg/TOC preservation in sedimentary records
Improve characterization of organic matter properties (source, maturation, oxic degradation) in future studies to enable better re-evaluation of Hg/TOC data
Investigate mechanisms by which different NAIP emplacement styles (flood basalt volcanism vs. submarine sill intrusions) produce distinct Hg and carbon isotope signals
Apply high-resolution geochronological dating to better constrain the timing relationships between volcanic activity, NAIP emplacement pulses, and climate changes
themes: Carbon cycle perturbations and greenhouse warming

10.1038/s41467-022-33390-x.title: Astrochronology of the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum on the Atlantic Coastal Plain
10.1038/s41467-022-33390-x.short_summary: This study develops an astrochronology for the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) using high-resolution calcium and magnetic susceptibility data from the Howards Tract cores on the Atlantic Coastal Plain. Statistical analyses confirm astronomical forcing, with results indicating the PETM carbon isotope excursion onset lasted approximately 6,000 years. The findings suggest the event was triggered during an extreme in orbital precession coupled with high eccentricity, supporting a role for astronomical forcing. This work challenges very rapid onset hypotheses and provides refined constraints on the timing and potential triggers of this ancient hyperthermal, offering insights relevant to understanding modern carbon cycle perturbations.
key_findings: The PETM carbon isotope excursion (CIE) onset lasted approximately 6 kyr based on astrochronological analysis of the Howards Tract cores, contrasting with earlier estimates ranging from 13 years to 20,000 years

Statistical evaluation using COCO and ASM methods confirms astronomical forcing with significance levels less than 0.05, with confidence levels exceeding 98.6% for astronomically forced variations in calcium and magnetic susceptibility
The PETM CIE onset occurred at an extreme in precession during a maximum in eccentricity cycles, suggesting orbital forcing played a role in triggering this global warming event

Carbonate saturation overshoot occurred during the PETM recovery phase, with calcium content increasing from 1.9% during the PETM body to 9.9% in the Nanjemoy Formation, indicating global ocean acidification followed by carbonate oversaturation

The Howards Tract paleoshelf record preserves approximately 110 kyr of the PETM interval with high sedimentation rates (~10 cm/kyr), providing exceptional temporal resolution compared to deep-sea records complicated by dissolution and bioturbation
10.1038/s41467-022-33390-x.methodology: Time series analysis (Lomb-Scargle spectral analysis, COCO, and ASM methods) of high-resolution proxy records (calcium content via XRF scanning and magnetic susceptibility at 5 mm resolution) from two offset drill cores at Howards Tract, Maryland. Results were validated using cGENIE Earth system modeling with transient astronomical forcing to simulate paleoclimate changes and examine links between orbital forcing and proxy oscillations.
limitations: The definition of the CIE onset at Howards Tract is complicated by early diagenetic carbonate (siderite) formation, which amplifies the global carbon isotope signal (~4-5‰) to ~13‰
Uncertainty exists regarding whether the more abrupt CIE onset at Howards Tract compared to South Dover Bridge reflects a condensed basal Marlboro Clay interval or diagenetic artifacts

The assumption of constant sedimentation rate within the first precession cycle may overestimate the CIE onset duration if significant acceleration occurred during transition from the Aquia Formation to the Marlboro Clay
The PETM record at Howards Tract is truncated by an unconformity at the top of the Marlboro Clay, preventing analysis of the complete event recovery phase
Statistical tuning approaches rely on objective methods but cannot eliminate all sources of uncertainty inherent in paleoclimate proxy interpretation
future_work: Conduct independent astrochronologic studies at additional coastal/shelf sites with high sedimentation rates to further constrain PETM onset duration and validate the 6 kyr estimate
Improve understanding of how regional versus global climate responses affected carbonate preservation patterns during the PETM in different depositional environments
Investigate whether similar orbital configurations (eccentricity maxima combined with precession extrema) triggered other hyperthermal events throughout the early Eocene
Develop better methods to deconvolve early diagenetic effects from original paleoclimate signals in shelf environments, particularly regarding siderite formation and isotope signatures
Extend cGENIE modeling to simulate carbon release scenarios concurrent with astronomical forcing to directly compare modeled carbon isotope excursions with observations from coastal shelf records
themes: Paleoclimate Proxies and Earth System Modeling

10.1126/sciadv.abh4224.title: Lithium isotope evidence for enhanced weathering and erosion during the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum
10.1126/sciadv.abh4224.short_summary: This study uses lithium isotopes from marine carbonates and detrital shales to investigate changes in silicate weathering and erosion during the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM). It finds a negative lithium isotope excursion, indicating a global shift toward more congruent weathering and a two- to threefold increase in physical erosion rates. This enhanced weathering and erosion regime promoted greater drawdown of atmospheric CO₂ through increased burial of both marine carbonate and organic carbon. The work is significant because it provides direct geochemical evidence linking intensified hydrological cycling during rapid warming to the feedback mechanisms that facilitated climatic recovery.
key_findings: A negative lithium isotope excursion (LIE) of ~3-4‰ was recorded in seawater during the PETM across multiple global carbonate sections, representing a shift from ~26.6‰ to 23-24‰
Global erosion rates increased by 2-3× over 100 ka during the PETM, with modeled weathering increases of 50-60% compared to pre-warming values

The observed lithium isotope changes require both increased riverine Li flux and decreased riverine δ⁷Li values, indicating a shift to lower weathering intensity (higher erosion relative to chemical weathering)
Enhanced weathering and erosion during the PETM supported increased carbon burial through both carbonate precipitation and organic carbon sequestration, promoting climatic recovery
Clay particle delivery to coastal oceans by erosion enhanced organic carbon burial rates, with peaks in local organic carbon burial coinciding with modeled maximum erosion rates
10.1126/sciadv.abh4224.methodology: Lithium isotope ratios (δ⁷Li) were analyzed from multiple marine carbonate sections (ODP sites 865, 1051, 1210) and detrital shale sections (Svalbard and Fur, Denmark) using ICP-MS methods. Dynamic box models incorporating outputs from two Earth System models (cGENIE EMIC and LOSCAR) were used to model seawater Li isotope evolution and constrain changes in silicate weathering rates and erosion regimes during the PETM.
limitations: Detrital Li isotope signals in shales are likely diluted by previously formed clay minerals eroded from continents, representing minimum excursion values
Global erosion rate estimates are derived from local sedimentation records which are heterogeneous and cannot directly estimate global changes
Uncertainty in carbonate fractionation factors (±1.5‰ for foraminifera) propagates significant uncertainty into modeled riverine δ⁷Li values
LOSCAR model requires physically unrealistic 12‰ decrease in riverine δ⁷Li over very short timescales (<10 ka) to match observations
Long oceanic Li residence time (~1 Ma) buffers the recovery signal, limiting precise determination of weathering change duration
future_work: Test whether clay dissolution from eroded Cretaceous laterites contributed significantly to the Li isotope excursion
Improve understanding of temperature-dependent lithium isotope fractionation during clay formation to better constrain paleoclimate reconstructions
Develop higher-resolution paleoclimate models that better capture extreme precipitation events and their relationship to erosion rates
Extend Li isotope records to other paleoclimate transitions to test whether coupled changes in Li flux and isotope ratio are universal features of warming events
Investigate the mechanisms linking enhanced erosion to organic carbon burial and quantify the relative contributions of carbonate versus organic carbon to CO₂ drawdown

10.1029/2008gc002116.title: Depth dependency of the Paleocene‐Eocene carbon isotope excursion: Paired benthic and terrestrial biomarker records (Ocean Drilling Program Leg 208, Walvis Ridge)
10.1029/2008gc002116.short_summary: This study investigates the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) carbon isotope excursion (CIE) using benthic foraminifera and terrestrial biomarkers from a depth transect on Walvis Ridge. It finds the recorded CIE magnitude in marine carbonate decreases with greater paleodepth (from -3.5‰ at ~1500 m to -1.5‰ at ~3600 m), demonstrating that deep-sea records are truncated by dissolution. The stronger excursion at shallower sites, supported by covarying terrestrial n-alkane data (up to -5‰), suggests the true deep-ocean CIE was likely at least -3.5‰. This implies prior underestimates of light carbon release and highlights how dissolution biases paleoceanographic reconstructions.
key_findings: The benthic foraminiferal carbon isotope excursion (CIE) is depth-dependent, showing -3.5‰ at 1500 m paleodepth (Site 1263) versus -1.5‰ at 3600 m paleodepth (Site 1262), consistent with depth-dependent carbonate dissolution truncation
Strong covariance between benthic foraminifera records and terrestrial n-alkane biomarkers from the same core (Site 1263) supports a rapid CIE of at least -3.5‰ but no more than -5.0‰

The CIE in benthic foraminifera is relatively abrupt rather than gradual as portrayed in bulk carbonate records, indicating that the gradual pattern in bulk carbonate is an artifact of dissolution and reworking
Site 1263 records the most complete benthic deep-sea PETM sequence with the lowest minimum δ13C value (-2.2‰) ever observed at any deep-sea site, substantially lower than Site 690
Mass balance calculations indicate that a global deep-sea CIE of at least -3.5‰ requires release of a minimum of 2300 GtC from methane hydrates (δ13C = -60‰) or up to 3300 GtC for a -5‰ excursion
10.1029/2008gc002116.methodology: This study analyzed stable carbon and oxygen isotopes from benthic foraminifera (Nuttallides truempyi and Oridorsalis umbonatus) sampled at 1 cm intervals across the PETM at three Ocean Drilling Program sites along a Walvis Ridge depth transect (Sites 1262, 1263, and 1265) at paleodepths of 3600, 1500, and 1850 m respectively. Results were compared with terrestrial n-alkane biomarker carbon isotope data from Site 1263 and X-ray fluorescence scanning data to establish chronostratigraphy and evaluate carbonate dissolution effects.
limitations: Data gaps in benthic foraminiferal records due to carbonate-free layers (13 cm at Site 1263, 38 cm at Site 1262)
Small, thin-walled foraminiferal specimens during the CIE made single-specimen isotopic analysis difficult in most cases
Possible overestimation of terrestrial CIE magnitude due to differential fractionation between angiosperms and gymnosperms and changes in plant community composition at high CO2 levels
Lack of palynological data for Walvis Ridge cores prevents definitive constraint of terrestrial biomarker artifacts
Chronostratigraphic uncertainties in absolute age assignment (55.53 Ma) due to ongoing refinement of orbital calibrations
future_work: Testing the hypothesis of a transient switch in deep-water circulation introducing isotopically light, warm, low-oxygen intermediate water mass to the south Atlantic
Constraining the floral composition and plant community changes during the PETM in West South Africa using palynological data to improve interpretation of terrestrial n-alkane records
Establishing more geographically distributed depth transects to better constrain the global three-dimensional structure of the CIE and carbon cycle perturbations
Refining absolute chronostratigraphy for the Paleocene-Eocene boundary through continued orbital calibration studies
Evaluating whether other proposed PETM carbon sources (flood basalt volcanism, thermal combustion of organic matter) could have worked in concert with methane hydrate dissociation

10.1126/sciadv.abg1025.title: Surface ocean warming and acidification driven by rapid carbon release precedes Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum
10.1126/sciadv.abg1025.short_summary: This study presents high-resolution geochemical evidence from Maryland shelf sediments revealing a rapid pre-onset excursion (POE) before the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM). The POE featured a 1.0–1.5‰ negative carbon isotope shift, ~2°C sea surface warming, and concurrent ocean acidification, indicating a separate, short-lived pulse of isotopically light carbon. Modeling suggests this precursor release was smaller and faster than the main PETM carbon injection but operated on a timescale more analogous to modern anthropogenic emissions. The findings significantly refine our understanding of carbon cycle dynamics during this major hyperthermal and provide a deeper geologic context for assessing current climate change.
key_findings: A 1.0 to 1.5‰ negative carbon isotope excursion (pre-onset excursion, POE) occurs before the main PETM carbon isotope excursion, with surface ocean warming of at least 2°C and ocean acidification (pH decline of 0.08 to 0.35)
The POE represents rapid carbon release (~400-1600 Gt C) over centuries to millennia, released into atmosphere and surface ocean reservoirs, distinct from the larger main PETM carbon input

Foraminifera boron isotope (δ11B) analysis using novel laser ablation techniques reveals unprecedented high-resolution pH records from shallow marine sediments that better preserve short-lived events than deep-sea cores
The POE is recorded in both marine and terrestrial records globally, confirming it as a distinct carbon injection event before the main phase of the PETM

The POE recovery occurred rapidly through mixing with the deep ocean reservoir (millennial timescales), while the main CIE required long-term carbon cycle feedbacks (tens of thousands of years) for recovery
10.1126/sciadv.abg1025.methodology: This study combines geochemical analysis of foraminifera from shallow marine sediments in Maryland (South Dover Bridge and Cambridge-Dorchester cores) with carbon cycle modeling using LOSCAR. Researchers measured stable isotopes (δ13C, δ18O) and trace elements (Mg/Ca, B/Ca) on planktonic and benthic foraminifera, and innovatively applied laser ablation MC-ICP-MS to analyze boron isotopes in individual benthic foraminifera specimens to reconstruct seawater pH with unprecedented temporal resolution during the Paleocene-Eocene boundary.
limitations: Low abundance of foraminifera in siliciclastic-dominated shelf sediments limits applicability of some geochemical proxies and reduces sampling resolution

High spatiotemporal variability in shelf deposition makes direct duration estimates uncertain; POE duration estimates range from 40 to over 200 ka at Maryland sites compared to <2 ka from terrestrial records
Large uncertainties associated with laser ablation δ11B measurements (±0.9-1.3‰ at 95% confidence) versus traditional solution methods (0.2-0.3‰), limiting absolute pH magnitude precision
Coarse biostratigraphic age control in the Aquia Formation makes correlation between shelf sites challenging and prevents splicing of continuous composite records
Limited resolution of δ11B records due to low foraminiferal abundance and variable seasonal/bioturbation effects makes precise timing comparison between pH and carbon isotope excursions difficult
future_work: Improved constraints on late Paleocene to early Eocene seawater boron isotope composition (δ11Bsw) to refine ocean pH estimates and reduce modeling uncertainties
Higher resolution sampling and additional core sites to better establish whether the POE is truly global and to improve chronostratigraphic correlation across regions

Investigation of specific carbon sources for the POE and CIE through additional geochemical tracers and evaluation of competing hypotheses (volcanic emissions, methane hydrate dissociation, permafrost thawing)
Examination of whether multiple carbon pulses represent elevated climate instability and positive feedback mechanisms preceding the PETM
Development of improved age models for shelf depositional sequences to better constrain POE duration and rates of carbon release
themes: Carbon cycle perturbations and carbon isotope excursions
Rapid climate change analogs to anthropogenic emissions

10.5194/cp-17-1989-2021.title: Rapid and sustained environmental responses to global warming: the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum in the eastern North Sea
10.5194/cp-17-1989-2021.short_summary: This study investigates environmental changes during the Paleocene–Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) using high-resolution data from the eastern North Sea (Denmark). Key findings show that rapid warming intensified the hydrological cycle, increasing terrestrial runoff and silicate weathering—partly fueled by volcanic ash from the North Atlantic Igneous Province. The basin became anoxic to sulfidic during the PETM, coinciding with enhanced organic carbon burial. These processes represent active negative feedbacks (weathering and carbon sequestration) that helped modulate the carbon cycle and drive recovery. The work underscores the importance of shelf regions in recording climatic responses and acting as carbon sinks during hyperthermal events.
key_findings: Kaolinite and chemical alteration indices show a rapid influx of terrestrial material shortly after the PETM onset, indicating an intensified hydrological cycle and enhanced erosion from deeply weathered Fennoscandian bedrock

Volcanically-derived zeolite (up to 36% of bulk mineralogy) and smectite minerals (up to 90% of clay fraction) dominated the sediments, highlighting the North Atlantic Igneous Province's role as a major sediment source and in accelerating silicate weathering during the PETM

Anoxic to sulfidic bottom-water conditions became widespread from the PETM onset onwards, with evidence including laminated sediments, high molybdenum enrichment factors (>30), elevated sulfur concentrations (~4 wt%), and pyrite accumulation (~7% of bulk)

Enhanced export productivity and organic-matter burial intensified in the upper PETM body, reaching maximum TOC values of 3.9 wt%, suggesting nutrient input from volcanic ash fertilization and contributing to rapid carbon sequestration and PETM recovery

Negative feedback mechanisms including silicate weathering and organic carbon sequestration responded rapidly to temperature and carbon cycle changes and remained active throughout the entire PETM interval
10.5194/cp-17-1989-2021.methodology: Multi-proxy sedimentological, mineralogical (XRD bulk and clay fraction), and geochemical analysis (XRF core scanning, Rock-Eval pyrolysis, ICP-MS, elemental analysis) of an expanded marine section at Stolleklint, Fur, Denmark spanning the PETM onset, body, and recovery phases (~55.9 Ma). High-resolution sampling across the PETM onset (1 cm intervals) and box-core collection for detailed elemental core scans.
limitations: Glaciotectonic deformation complicates stratigraphic thickness estimates at the study site
The exact timing of basin deoxygenation onset is somewhat blurred by extensive ash deposition in the pre-PETM sediments
Limited evidence of visible ash layers during the PETM body, though cryptotephras may be present but require more detailed confirmation
Paleocene-Eocene boundary exposure was not available at the time of fieldwork due to coastal erosion, necessitating excavation of a beach trench
Clay mineral assemblages are an unreliable paleoclimate proxy at sub-million-year resolution due to slow soil formation and long lag times between formation and marine deposition
Data cannot definitively determine the relative contribution of silicate weathering versus terrestrial carbon sequestration in driving PETM recovery
future_work: More detailed work needed to confirm the presence and significance of cryptotephra deposits during the PETM body interval
Investigation of the relative timing of volcanic activity and climatic/environmental changes to better constrain the NAIP's causal relationship to PETM initiation and termination
Further constraints on the global extent and timing of enhanced silicate weathering during the PETM to understand its role in carbon drawdown
Expanded studies from additional North Sea shelf sections to better track regional variations in environmental response to PETM climatic changes
High-resolution geochemical and biomarker analysis to better distinguish between productivity-driven versus weathering-driven mechanisms of carbon sequestration during PETM recovery
themes: Volcanic ash and North Atlantic Igneous Province
Marine productivity and organic carbon burial

10.1029/2006eo170002.title: Eocene hyperthermal event offers insight into greenhouse warming
10.1029/2006eo170002.short_summary: This document examines the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) as a key geological analogue for modern anthropogenic climate change. During the PETM, roughly 55 million years ago, a rapid release of 1500–4500 gigatons of carbon caused global warming of 5–9°C, ocean acidification, major shifts in hydrology and ecosystems, and a significant benthic extinction. Research reveals a three-phase Earth system response: initiation, a prolonged altered state, and eventual recovery. The event underscores the profound, interconnected impacts of massive carbon release in a warm world. A separate section introduces the FORMOSAT-3/COSMIC satellite constellation, designed to monitor global atmospheric and ionospheric conditions.
key_findings: During the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) ~55 million years ago, global temperatures warmed by 5-9°C globally due to release of 1500-4500 gigatons of carbon, comparable to anticipated 21st century carbon addition

The PETM demonstrates a three-phase Earth systems response: Phase I initiation with abrupt biotic and geochemical changes, Phase II an alternate semistable state lasting ~60,000 years with distinctive biotic assemblages, and Phase III recovery over ~70,000 years

Benthic foraminiferal extinction during PETM was the most severe in the last 90 million years, with loss of 35-50% of deep-sea benthic foraminiferal species, while terrestrial mammals dispersed intercontinentally with body size reductions

Ocean circulation may have shifted during the PETM, with reduced pole-to-equator temperature gradients potentially moving deep-water formation from Southern Ocean to subtropical or Northern Hemisphere latitudes, though evidence remains debated
Weathering feedbacks and increased marine carbonate burial during PETM recovery indicate the geosphere's critical role in carbon cycle stabilization following massive carbon release
10.1029/2006eo170002.methodology: The authors synthesize paleoceanographic, paleoclimatic, and paleobiological records from multiple terrestrial and marine study sites to reconstruct Earth systems changes during the PETM. They integrate carbon isotope records, temperature proxies, biotic assemblage data, and ocean circulation indicators to develop a three-phase model of perturbation and recovery.
limitations: Deep-water circulation reconstructions based on benthic foraminifera carbon isotope records are geographically sparse and may be influenced by local organic-matter regeneration
Dissolution at PETM onset creates time gaps that make precise site-to-site correlation difficult or impossible
Low δ13C values characteristic of the PETM body remain unexplained, limiting understanding of carbon cycle dynamics during this interval
The trigger for initial PETM carbon release remains unresolved—whether feedback-driven or externally forced
Direct versus indirect ecosystem responses to carbon release (ocean acidification vs. temperature and precipitation changes) are not completely understood
future_work: Additional focused study of the PETM 'body' interval to characterize the alternate semistable state that may represent future global environmental conditions
Further investigation of how marine carbonate burial recovery proceeded after 60,000 years of low burial rates during the PETM body
Determination of whether the PETM was initiated as a climate feedback or external forcing event to assess applicability as modern climate analogue
Improved understanding of linkages between carbon cycle perturbation and synchronous biotic change across ecosystems
Development of better tools for reconstructing deep-ocean circulation to resolve contradictions between different proxy records
themes: Carbon cycle perturbation and greenhouse warming

10.1029/2000pa000541.title: An osmium isotope excursion associated with the Late Paleocene thermal maximum: Evidence of intensified chemical weathering
10.1029/2000pa000541.short_summary: This study documents a transient ~10% increase in the seawater ¹⁸⁷Os/¹⁸⁸Os ratio during the Late Paleocene Thermal Maximum (LPTM), a brief period of extreme global warming ~55 million years ago. The authors argue this isotopic excursion, recorded at two widely separated ocean sites and correlated with a negative carbon isotope excursion, reflects a globally significant increase in continental chemical weathering driven by warm, humid conditions. The findings support the operation of a negative feedback mechanism where warming accelerates silicate weathering, drawing down atmospheric CO₂ and ultimately stabilizing climate on 10⁴–10⁵ year timescales.
key_findings: A >10% increase in 187Os/188Os ratios was observed during the Late Paleocene Thermal Maximum (LPTM) at two widely separated sites (North Atlantic Site 549 and Indian Ocean Site 213), indicating a transient whole-ocean shift in osmium isotopic composition
The osmium isotope excursion is closely correlated with the carbon isotope excursion (negative δ13C values) defining the LPTM, suggesting a common causative mechanism

The 23-26% increase in continental osmium flux to seawater inferred from osmium isotope data is consistent with laboratory-measured activation energies for silicate weathering (approximately 30 kJ/mol) given the 4-7°C warming during the LPTM

Independent evidence supports accelerated chemical weathering during the LPTM including: increased kaolinite abundances, climate model predictions of increased precipitation, and nannofossil morphological changes consistent with decreased surface water density

The osmium isotope record exhibits a coherent response to climate extremes: excursions to higher 187Os/188Os during warm/humid periods (LPTM) and lower ratios during cold/arid glacial episodes, supporting a weathering-based climate feedback mechanism operating on 10^4-10^5 year timescales
10.1029/2000pa000541.methodology: Osmium isotope analyses were performed on bulk sediments from two DSDP sites using magnetic sector inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), with NiS fire assay preconcentration. High-resolution sampling across the LPTM event captured detailed variations in 187Os/188Os ratios correlated with carbon and oxygen isotope data from the same samples.
limitations: Low osmium concentration samples from Site 213 had procedural blank corrections as large as 12%, limiting precision and accuracy
The study cannot definitively rule out changes in the 187Os/188Os ratio of riverine input as a contributor to the observed seawater osmium excursion
Difficulty in constraining the exact duration of the LPTM due to incomplete stratigraphic records at individual sites
Limited number of high-resolution osmium isotope datasets available for comparison, making it difficult to assess how common abrupt excursions in the marine Os record are

The interpretation that increased continental weathering caused the osmium excursion must be considered tentative and depends on the assumption that riverine 187Os/188Os flux did not change significantly during the LPTM
future_work: Additional high-resolution osmium isotope datasets needed to better characterize the frequency and nature of osmium excursions in marine records and establish the method's broader applicability
Better constraints on the processes responsible for osmium isotope variations during short climatic events through studies of multiple well-characterized thermal episodes

Late Paleocene thermal maximum (LPTM) and early Eocene climate

10.1146/annurev-earth-040610-133431.title: The Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum: A Perturbation of Carbon Cycle, Climate, and Biosphere with Implications for the Future
10.1146/annurev-earth-040610-133431.venue: Annual Review of Earth and Planetary Sciences
10.1146/annurev-earth-040610-133431.short_summary: During the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM), ∼56 Mya, thousands of petagrams of carbon were released into the ocean-atmosphere system with attendant changes in the carbon cycle, climate, ocean chemistry, and marine and continental ecosystems. The period of carbon release is thought to have lasted <20 ka, the duration of the whole event was ∼200 ka, and the global temperature increase was 5–8°C. Terrestrial and marine organisms experienced large shifts in geographic ranges, rapid evolut
key_findings: The PETM involved massive carbon release (thousands of petagrams) into the ocean-atmosphere system over a short period (<20 ka), causing global temperature increases of 5-8°C
Organisms experienced significant geographic range shifts, rapid evolution, and trophic ecology changes, with benthic foraminifera being the primary group to suffer major extinctions

The PETM serves as a paleoclimate analog for understanding carbon cycle perturbations, climate system responses, and biotic responses to rapid environmental change relevant to future global warming scenarios
10.1146/annurev-earth-040610-133431.methodology: The study synthesizes paleoclimate and paleontological evidence from the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum period (~56 Mya) to examine changes in carbon cycling, climate, ocean chemistry, and ecosystem responses across terrestrial and marine environments over a ~200 ka timeframe.
limitations: Limited to paleoclimate proxy data from ~56 million years ago
Temporal resolution constraints may limit precise dating of carbon release mechanisms
Extinction patterns vary significantly across different taxonomic groups, complicating generalizations about biotic responses
future_work: Refining understanding of carbon release mechanisms and sources during the PETM
Improving paleoclimate models to better predict biotic responses to rapid environmental change
Using PETM analogs to inform projections of future climate and ecosystem impacts from anthropogenic carbon release

10.1029/2021gl097436.title: Swift Weathering Response on Floodplains During the Paleocene‐Eocene Thermal Maximum
10.1029/2021gl097436.short_summary: Abstract Silicate weathering is thought to increase and offset the rapid, massive input of CO 2 into the atmosphere and ocean during the Paleocene‐Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM), but few nonmarine records have been used to quantify this. We probe changes in silicate weathering intensity by measuring Li isotope ratios of bedrock and ancient floodplain deposits spanning the PETM in the Bighorn Basin, Wyoming (USA). Our results reveal a rapid increase in silicate weathering intensity during the PETM
key_findings: Silicate weathering intensity increased rapidly during the PETM and remained elevated during initial climate recovery, as revealed through lithium isotope analysis
Soils formed farther from river channels experienced larger weathering changes than near-channel soils, suggesting distance from water sources influences weathering response
Increased seasonal fluctuations in water table height on floodplains, combined with elevated temperatures and pCO2, drove the enhanced silicate weathering response
10.1029/2021gl097436.methodology: Lithium isotope ratios were measured from bedrock and ancient floodplain deposits spanning the PETM in the Bighorn Basin, Wyoming to quantify changes in silicate weathering intensity across spatial locations and temporal intervals.
limitations: Limited to nonmarine floodplain records from a single geographic location (Bighorn Basin)
Focuses on initial stage of climate recovery with temporal coverage constraints
future_work: Expand nonmarine weathering records to other geographic regions to assess global patterns
Investigate longer-term weathering response throughout the full PETM climate recovery period
Examine mechanisms driving differential weathering responses between distal and proximal floodplain positions

10.1029/95pa02087.title: Dissociation of oceanic methane hydrate as a cause of the carbon isotope excursion at the end of the Paleocene
10.1029/95pa02087.short_summary: Isotopic records across the “Latest Paleocene Thermal Maximum“ (LPTM) indicate that bottom water temperature increased by more than 4°C during a brief time interval (<10 4 years) of the latest Paleocene (∼55.6 Ma). There also was a coeval −2 to −3‰ excursion in the δ 13 C of the ocean/atmosphere inorganic carbon reservoir. Given the large mass of this reservoir, a rapid δ 13 C shift of this magnitude is difficult to explain within the context of conventional hypotheses for changing the mean c
key_findings: Dissociation of oceanic methane hydrate during the Latest Paleocene Thermal Maximum (LPTM) can explain the observed -2 to -3‰ carbon isotope excursion in the ocean/atmosphere inorganic carbon reservoir

Bottom water temperature increased by >4°C over a brief interval (<10,000 years) at ~55.6 Ma, creating thermal conditions sufficient to destabilize oceanic methane hydrates at intermediate water depths
Release and oxidation of 1.1 to 2.1 × 10^18 g of carbon from methane hydrate (δ13C ≈ -60‰) is sufficient to produce the observed paleoceanographic carbon isotope excursion
10.1029/95pa02087.methodology: The study uses isotopic records (δ13C measurements) and paleoceanographic data from the Latest Paleocene Thermal Maximum to model the thermal dissociation of oceanic methane hydrate reservoirs and calculate the carbon flux required to explain the observed carbon isotope shift.
limitations: Analysis based on present-day oceanic methane hydrate reservoir estimates, which may differ from Paleocene conditions
Focus on direct thermal dissociation mechanism without detailed discussion of alternative carbon sources or competing hypotheses
future_work: Development of comprehensive climate and paleoceanographic models that incorporate methane hydrate dissociation during the LPTM
Further investigation of the fate and cycling of methane released from oceanic hydrates

10.1130/0091-7613(1997)025<0259:abogit>2.title: A blast of gas in the latest Paleocene: Simulating first-order effects of massive dissociation of oceanic methane hydrate
10.1130/0091-7613(1997)025<0259:abogit>2.short_summary: Carbonate and organic matter deposited during the latest Paleocene thermal maximum is characterized by a remarkable -2.5% excursion in delta 13C that occurred over approximately 10(4) yr and returned to near initial values in an exponential pattern over approximately 2 x 10(5) yr. It has been hypothesized that this excursion signifies transfer of 1.4 to 2.8 x 10(18) g of CH4 from oceanic hydrates to the combined ocean-atmosphere inorganic carbon reservoir. A scenario with 1.12 x 10(18) g of CH4
key_findings: Massive dissociation of oceanic methane hydrate (~1.12 x 10^18 g) during the latest Paleocene can explain the observed δ13C excursion of -2.5% that occurred over 10,000 years
Methane release from oceanic hydrates causes a shoaling of the lysocline by up to 400 meters and increases global surface temperature by approximately 2°C over 10,000 years
The magnitude of CH4 flux during the Paleocene thermal maximum is comparable to present-day anthropogenic methane sources, with implications for understanding modern climate change
10.1130/0091-7613(1997)025<0259:abogit>2.methodology: Numerical simulation of methane hydrate dissociation within the framework of the present-day global carbon cycle model to test the plausibility of the hypothesis that oceanic hydrate destabilization caused the observed paleoclimatic signals during the latest Paleocene thermal maximum.

10.1073/pnas.1321177111.title: Onset of carbon isotope excursion at the Paleocene-Eocene thermal maximum took millennia, not 13 years
10.1073/pnas.1321177111.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
10.1073/pnas.1321177111.short_summary: The Paleocene-Eocene thermal maximum (PETM) may represent the best paleo-analog for rapid and massive carbon release to the ocean and atmosphere. Thus, constraining the carbon release rate at its onset is critical. Wright and Schaller (1) use records from apparently rhythmically layered shelf sediments to argue that the layering is annual and that the onset of the carbon isotope excursion (CIE, fingerprint for carbon release) in the surface ocean was complete in 13 y. Using basic carbon cycle an
key_findings: The onset of the carbon isotope excursion (CIE) at the Paleocene-Eocene thermal maximum (PETM) took at least several millennia, not 13 years as previously argued
Carbon cycle and climate considerations demonstrate that a 13-year timeline for carbon release onset is physically infeasible
The timescale findings rule out a cometary origin as the source of the carbon release during the PETM
10.1073/pnas.1321177111.methodology: The authors use carbon cycle modeling and climate considerations to reanalyze isotope records from rhythmically layered shelf sediments and challenge previous interpretations of rapid carbon release timing.
limitations: Analysis is limited to existing isotope records from shelf sediments
The study does not provide absolute constraints on the maximum duration of CIE onset, only establishing a minimum of several millennia
future_work: Further refinement of paleoclimate records to better constrain the exact duration of carbon release at the PETM onset
Investigation of alternative carbon sources to cometary impacts that could explain the observed CIE pattern

10.1126/science.1135274.title: Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum and the Opening of the Northeast Atlantic
10.1126/science.1135274.short_summary: The Paleocene-Eocene thermal maximum (PETM) has been attributed to a sudden release of carbon dioxide and/or methane. 40 Ar/ 39 Ar age determinations show that the Danish Ash-17 deposit, which overlies the PETM by about 450,000 years in the Atlantic, and the Skraenterne Formation Tuff, representing the end of 1 ± 0.5 million years of massive volcanism in East Greenland, are coeval. The relative age of Danish Ash-17 thus places the PETM onset after the beginning of massive flood basalt volcanism
key_findings: The Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) was triggered by greenhouse gas release from magma interaction with carbon-rich sedimentary rocks during the opening of the Northeast Atlantic
40Ar/39Ar dating demonstrates that massive flood basalt volcanism in East Greenland at 56.1 ± 0.4 million years ago is temporally linked to the PETM and continental breakup events
The PETM onset coincides with the beginning of flood basalt volcanism and is within dating error of the continental breakup time marked by mid-ocean ridge basalt eruptions
10.1126/science.1135274.methodology: The study employs 40Ar/39Ar radiometric dating of volcanic deposits (Danish Ash-17 and Skraenterne Formation Tuff) to establish precise chronological correlations between volcanism, continental breakup, and the PETM, using stratigraphic relationships to constrain the timing of events.
limitations: The dating relies on correlations between Atlantic and Greenland deposits separated by approximately 450,000 years
Uncertainty in dating constrains allow for alternative interpretations within error margins
future_work: Further investigation of carbon-rich sedimentary rocks at the embryonic plate boundary to quantify greenhouse gas release
Higher resolution dating studies to distinguish between competing mechanisms of PETM initiation

10.1126/science.285.5428.724.title: Mechanisms of Climate Warming at the End of the Paleocene
10.1126/science.285.5428.724.short_summary: An abrupt episode of global warming marked the end of the Paleocene epoch. Oxygen and carbon isotope records from two widely separated sites support the notion that degassing of biogenic methane hydrate may have been an important factor in altering Earth's climate. The data show evidence for multiple injections of methane, separated by intervals in which the carbon cycle was in stasis. Correlations between the two sites suggest that even these small-scale events were global in nature.
key_findings: Abrupt global warming at the end of the Paleocene epoch was driven by degassing of biogenic methane hydrate
Multiple discrete methane injection events occurred, separated by periods of stable carbon cycling
Small-scale methane release events had global climatic impacts despite their localized origins
10.1126/science.285.5428.724.methodology: The study employs oxygen and carbon isotope records from two geographically distant sites to track climate changes and methane release events during the late Paleocene, using isotopic correlations to establish global-scale synchronicity of warming events.
limitations: Analysis limited to two widely separated sites
Isotope records may not capture all methane release mechanisms
Temporal resolution of isotopic data may obscure rapid climate dynamics
future_work: Expand isotopic sampling to additional globally distributed sites for robust paleoclimate reconstruction
Investigate the mechanisms triggering successive methane hydrate destabilization events
Develop higher-resolution chronologies to better constrain the timing and duration of warming episodes

Panchuk 1Department of Geosciences, Pennsylvania State University, University Park, Pennsylvania 16802, USA Search for other works by this author on: GSW Google Scholar A. Ridgwell; A. Ridgwell 2School of Geographical Sciences, University of Bristol, University Road, Bristol BS8 1SS, UK Search for other works by this author on: GSW Google Scholar L.R. K
key_findings: 6800 Pg C (δ13C of –22‰) is the minimum carbon pulse needed to reproduce observed seafloor CaCO3 dissolution patterns
Methane hydrate destabilization alone cannot account for the carbon release; volcanic CO2, organic carbon oxidation, and thermogenic methane must have contributed

10.1130/g24474a.1.methodology: The study uses an intermediate complexity Earth-system model configured with early Eocene paleogeography to simulate carbon release scenarios and compare model outputs with observed sedimentary dissolution records from the Paleocene-Eocene boundary.

10.22498/pages.16.1.9.title: Carbon burp and transient global warming during the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum
10.22498/pages.16.1.9.short_summary: The Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM), ~55.5 Myr ago, was a geologically brief (~170 kyr) episode of globally elevated temperatures that occurred superimposed on the long-term late Paleocene and early Eocene warming trend.It was marked by a 5-8°C warming in both lowand high-latitude regions, a perturbation of the hydrological cycle and a major biotic response on land and in the oceans, including radiations, extinctions and migrations (see overviews in Bowen et al., 2006; Sluijs et al., 200
key_findings: The Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) ~55.5 million years ago was a brief (~170 kyr) episode of globally elevated temperatures (5-8°C warming) marked by a pronounced negative carbon isotope excursion (CIE) indicating a massive injection of 13C-depleted carbon into the ocean-atmosphere system

The carbon 'burp' involved at least 1.5×10^18 g (1500 Gt) of carbon, with ongoing research focusing on whether this carbon caused the warming or acted as a positive feedback mechanism in an already warming world
The PETM triggered major biotic responses including radiations, extinctions, and migrations in both terrestrial and marine ecosystems, along with perturbations to the hydrological cycle
10.22498/pages.16.1.9.methodology: The study employs paleoclimate reconstruction and stable carbon isotope analysis of sedimentary components to characterize the PETM event. The research examines geochemical signatures and fossil records to determine carbon source mechanisms and their relationship to global warming patterns.
limitations: The exact source and injection mechanism of the carbon burp remains unclear
Questions remain about causality versus feedback relationships between carbon injection and warming
Limited understanding of whether PETM was unique in the early Paleogene greenhouse world
future_work: Elucidating the precise source of the 13C-depleted carbon and its injection mechanisms
Determining whether the carbon injection directly caused the PETM warming or functioned as a positive feedback in an already warming world
Assessing the uniqueness of the PETM event within the early Paleogene climate context
Drawing comparisons between the fossil carbon burp of the PETM and modern anthropogenic carbon release from fossil fuel burning

clusters.label: Volcanic and Magmatic Carbon Sources
clusters.description: Research examining volcanism, particularly the North Atlantic Igneous Province (NAIP), as a primary trigger for the PETM through direct carbon emissions and indirect mechanisms. Studies investigate hydrothermal venting, thermogenic degassing from contact metamorphism of organic-rich sediments, magma-sediment interactions, volcanic CO2 release from Large Igneous Provinces, and the temporal correlation between massive flood basalt volcanism and PETM onset. This theme represents one of the two dominant hypotheses for the carbon source driving the PETM.
sub_themes: Shallow-water hydrothermal vent complexes
North Atlantic Igneous Province magmatism and timing
Thermogenic methane from contact metamorphism
Volcanic CO2 emissions from Large Igneous Provinces
Subcrustal carbon mobilization
Mercury as volcanic activity proxy
Comet impact vs. volcanism contributions
conflicts: Debate over whether volcanic CO2 alone is sufficient versus requiring thermogenic methane from sediment heating
Uncertainty in quantifying direct volcanic CO2 versus indirectly mobilized carbon
Comet impact study (10.1029/2019gl084818) suggests both impact and volcanism occurred, but impact carbon contribution was minimal
gaps: Precise quantification of carbon released through different volcanic mechanisms
Better constraints on timing of individual volcanic phases relative to CIE onset
Understanding of how volcanic carbon release rates compare to methane hydrate release rates

clusters.label: Methane Hydrate Dissociation and Positive Feedbacks
clusters.description: Research focused on methane hydrate destabilization as a major carbon source for the PETM, examining thermal dissociation mechanisms, heat transfer dynamics in marine sediments, and the plausibility of massive methane release from oceanic reservoirs. Studies address whether methane hydrates alone can explain the carbon isotope excursion, explore multi-stage methane release scenarios, and investigate pre-onset warming as evidence for positive feedback mechanisms. This theme also encompasses alternative sedimentary carbon sources including petrogenic organic carbon mobilization.
sub_themes: Thermal dissociation mechanisms of oceanic methane hydrates
Heat conduction and sediment temperature dynamics
Multiple methane injection events and two-stage models
Pre-onset warming and positive carbon cycle feedbacks
Petrogenic organic carbon oxidation
Barium burial as evidence for methane seepage
PETM duration explained by slow hydrate dissociation
conflicts: Whether methane hydrate reservoirs were large enough to explain the full CIE magnitude
Two-stage modeling (10.1029/2010gl046038) suggests Stage 1 results don't match any known PETM hypothesis
Debate over whether pre-onset warming triggered hydrates or hydrates caused initial warming
gaps: Quantification of Paleocene methane hydrate reservoir sizes and locations
Better constraints on isotopic composition of released methane
Understanding spatial heterogeneity in hydrate dissociation responses

clusters.label: Carbon Isotope Stratigraphy, Chronology, and Event Duration
clusters.description: High-resolution stratigraphic research documenting the timing, duration, and environmental context of the PETM through carbon isotope excursions and associated proxies. Studies address PETM onset duration (ranging from years to millennia), total event duration, recovery phases, pre-onset excursions (POE), astrochronology and orbital forcing, and spatial variability in carbon isotope records. This theme examines methodological challenges including substrate bias, dissolution effects, statistical uncertainty, and differences between terrestrial and marine records.
sub_themes: PETM duration estimates and astrochronology
Pre-onset excursion (POE) and multi-phase carbon release
Carbon isotope excursion magnitude and substrate effects
Depth-dependent dissolution truncation of records
Terrestrial vs. marine organic matter isotope signals
Orbital forcing and astronomical pacing
High-resolution coastal plain records
Statistical methods for uncertainty quantification
conflicts: Major disagreement on CIE onset duration: 13 years (rejected by 10.1073/pnas.1321177111) vs. 6 kyr (10.1038/s41467-022-33390-x) vs. longer estimates
Total PETM duration estimates range from ~120 kyr to ~270 kyr (10.1029/2024gl113117 argues for longer duration)
Differential fractionation between angiosperms and conifers (10.1016/j.epsl.2007.04.024) complicates terrestrial CIE interpretation
gaps: Reconciling different substrate records to determine true atmospheric CIE magnitude
Better understanding of how dissolution affects deep-sea carbonate records
Need for more high-resolution terrestrial records with good age control

clusters.label: Carbon Cycle Modeling and Source Quantification
clusters.description: Numerical modeling efforts to understand carbon sources, fluxes, and Earth system responses during the PETM. Research includes carbon cycle box models, Earth system models with coupled atmosphere-ocean-biosphere components, climate sensitivity assessments, and quantification of carbon release rates and total masses. Studies address fundamental questions about CO2 versus methane forcing, climate feedbacks, the sufficiency of different carbon sources to explain observations, and model-data comparisons to constrain emission scenarios.
sub_themes: Carbon source quantification and isotopic constraints
Earth system model simulations of PETM
Climate sensitivity to CO2 and CH4 forcing
Carbon cycle recovery timescales
Two-stage and multi-pulse carbon release scenarios
Model-data comparison and validation
Minimum carbon mass requirements from CaCO3 dissolution
Beyond methane: multiple carbon source theories
conflicts: CO2 forcing alone insufficient to explain warming (10.1038/ngeo578) vs. studies suggesting volcanic CO2 was dominant
Carbon mass estimates range from 1500 to >6800 Pg C depending on assumed isotopic composition
Methane hydrate dissociation alone cannot explain dissolution patterns (10.1130/g24474a.1)
gaps: Better constraints on Paleocene background climate state for modeling
Understanding of non-CO2 greenhouse gas contributions and feedbacks
Integration of volcanic, hydrate, and other carbon sources in comprehensive models

clusters.label: Ocean Biogeochemical Responses: Anoxia, Acidification, and Biotic Change
clusters.description: Research documenting marine environmental changes during the PETM, including ocean deoxygenation and development of euxinic conditions, ocean acidification and carbonate dissolution, productivity changes, and basin restriction effects. Studies examine spatial variability across different basins and water depths, connections between carbon cycle perturbations and marine redox conditions, sea level changes, and biotic responses including mass mortality events, ecosystem disruptions, and benthic foraminiferal extinctions. This theme bridges environmental consequences with biological impacts.
sub_themes: Ocean anoxia and euxinia development
Photic zone euxinia and biomarker evidence
Ocean acidification and pH reconstruction
Carbonate compensation depth changes and dissolution
Basin restriction and hydrographic changes
Marine productivity and organic carbon burial
Freshwater ecosystem collapse and mass mortalities
Dinoflagellate cyst assemblages as climate state proxies
Terrestrial and marine ecosystem disruptions
conflicts: Spatial heterogeneity in ocean redox responses - some basins show strong euxinia while others don't
Timing of anoxia onset relative to CIE varies between locations
gaps: Limited high-resolution records from open ocean settings
Better quantification of productivity changes and their role in carbon burial
Understanding teleconnections between different ocean basins during PETM

clusters.label: Silicate Weathering Feedbacks and Climate Recovery
clusters.description: Research examining negative feedback mechanisms through enhanced chemical weathering and physical erosion during the PETM that ultimately removed CO2 from the atmosphere and facilitated climate recovery. Studies employ lithium and osmium isotope proxies to track weathering intensity changes, document the coupling between carbon cycle perturbations and weathering responses, and investigate the role of volcanic ash in accelerating weathering. Research explores both marine and terrestrial weathering records across different latitudes and depositional environments including lacustrine and floodplain settings.
sub_themes: Lithium isotope records of weathering intensity
Osmium isotope evidence for continental weathering
Volcanic ash as weathering substrate
Hydrological cycle intensification and runoff
Floodplain and lacustrine weathering environments
Congruent vs. incongruent weathering during warming
Erosion rate increases during PETM
Carbon-weathering coupling on millennial timescales
conflicts: Magnitude of weathering increase varies between proxies and locations (50-100% increase estimated)
Relative importance of volcanic ash weathering vs. enhanced continental weathering
gaps: Quantifying total CO2 drawdown from enhanced weathering
Understanding lag times between warming and weathering response
Better constraints on weathering rates in different climate zones during PETM
